adults.option_b: They were absorbed into the kingdom of Israel as tribute-paying provinces
adults.option_c: They fled beyond the Euphrates and were never heard from again
adults.option_d: They surrendered their territory to Joab and withdrew to Assyria
adults.explanation: 2 Samuel 10:19 — "And when all the kings that were servants to Hadarezer saw that they were smitten before Israel, they made peace with Israel, and served them."

adults.question: What does Joab's exhortation to Abishai before battle reveal about Israelite military theology?
adults.option_a: He declared they would fight for their people and the cities of God and let the LORD do what seemed good to Him
adults.option_b: He told Abishai that numbers did not matter because the LORD would give victory
adults.option_c: He reminded Abishai of David's past victories as proof God would fight for them
adults.option_d: He said their covenant with God guaranteed them victory if they were faithful
adults.explanation: 2 Samuel 10:12 — "Be of good courage, and let us play the men for our people, and for the cities of our God: and the LORD do that which seemeth him good."

adults.question: What does the diplomatic incident in chapter 10 reveal about the dangers of misread intentions in international relations?
adults.option_a: Hanun's advisors misinterpreted a genuine act of hesed as espionage, turning a potential alliance into a costly war through paranoia and counsel of distrust
adults.option_b: It shows that Ammonite intelligence was superior to Israel's diplomatic code
adults.option_c: It reveals that David's empire was so threatening that even friendly overtures were suspected
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that ancient Near Eastern diplomacy required military escorts for all embassies
adults.explanation: 2 Samuel 10:3-4 — The princes' bad advice ("thinkest thou that David doth honour thy father?") led to needless war, showing how false counsel and distrust can destroy peace.

adults.question: How does chapter 10 function as a narrative transition toward the Bathsheba episode in chapter 11?
adults.option_a: It establishes the ongoing siege of Rabbah as the military context into which the Bathsheba narrative is inserted — "at the time when kings go forth to battle" David stays in Jerusalem
adults.option_b: It introduces Uriah the Hittite as a military hero before his death in chapter 11
adults.option_c: It shows David becoming complacent about his military duties as his empire expanded
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that David's enemies were all defeated before his greatest moral failure
adults.explanation: 2 Samuel 10:14 — "Joab returned from the children of Ammon, and came to Jerusalem." 2 Samuel 11:1 opens: "And it came to pass...at the time when kings go forth to battle...David tarried still at Jerusalem" — the unfinished Ammonite war provides the backdrop for David's fall.

kids.question: What did King David do while his army was at war?
kids.option_a: He stayed in Jerusalem and walked on his roof
kids.option_b: He led the army in battle
kids.option_c: He went to the temple to pray
kids.explanation: It came to pass that David tarried still at Jerusalem (2 Samuel 11:1), and he arose from his bed and walked upon the roof of the king's house (11:2).

kids.question: What was the name of the woman David saw from his roof?
kids.explanation: David saw a woman washing herself and inquired about her, and one said, "Is not this Bathsheba?" (2 Samuel 11:3).

kids.question: What happened to Uriah during the battle after David sent a letter to Joab?
kids.explanation: Joab placed Uriah in the hottest part of the battle, and the men of the city smote Uriah, and he died (2 Samuel 11:17).

teens.question: What time of year did David stay in Jerusalem while kings normally went to battle?
teens.option_a: At the time when kings go forth to battle in spring
teens.option_b: During the winter harvest
teens.option_c: At the feast of tabernacles
teens.option_d: During the feast of unleavened bread
teens.explanation: It came to pass, after the year was expired, at the time when kings go forth to battle, that David sent Joab (2 Samuel 11:1).

teens.question: Who was Bathsheba's husband?
teens.option_a: Uriah the Hittite
teens.option_b: Joab the commander
teens.option_c: Abner the son of Ner
teens.option_d: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada
teens.explanation: David inquired about the woman and was told, "Is not this Bathsheba, the wife of Uriah the Hittite?" (2 Samuel 11:3).

teens.question: Why did Uriah refuse to go home to his wife when David urged him?
teens.option_a: He said the ark and Israel and Judah abide in tents, so he could not eat, drink, and lie with his wife
teens.option_b: He was angry at David for summoning him
teens.option_c: He was ill and could not travel
teens.option_d: He had taken a vow of fasting
teens.explanation: Uriah said, "The ark, and Israel, and Judah, abide in tents...shall I then go into mine house?" (2 Samuel 11:11).

teens.question: What did David write in the letter he sent by Uriah's own hand to Joab?
teens.option_a: Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire from him, that he may be smitten
teens.option_b: Promote Uriah to be captain over the host
teens.option_c: Give Uriah leave to return home to his family
teens.option_d: Send Uriah back to Jerusalem immediately
teens.explanation: David wrote in the letter, "Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire ye from him, that he may be smitten, and die" (2 Samuel 11:15).

teens.question: What did David do after Bathsheba mourned for Uriah?
teens.option_a: He brought her to his house and she became his wife
teens.option_b: He sent her back to her father's house
teens.option_c: He appointed a guardian for her
teens.option_d: He had her taken to the temple
teens.explanation: When the mourning was past, David sent and fetched her to his house, and she became his wife (2 Samuel 11:27).

adults.question: Against what city was Joab fighting when Uriah was killed?
adults.option_a: Rabbah of the children of Ammon
adults.option_b: Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Hebron
adults.option_d: Gaza
adults.explanation: Joab besieged Rabbah; David sent Joab and all Israel to Rabbah (2 Samuel 11:1), and Uriah died at the siege of Rabbah (11:17).

adults.question: What was Bathsheba's message to David after their encounter?
adults.option_a: "I am with child"
adults.option_b: "My husband has returned from war"
adults.option_c: "Uriah knows what you have done"
adults.option_d: "Send me back to my father's house"
adults.explanation: Bathsheba conceived, and sent and told David, and said, "I am with child" (2 Samuel 11:5).

adults.question: What did David send to Uriah on the second night to try to get him to go home?
adults.option_a: He made him drunk with wine
adults.option_b: He gave him a gift of gold
adults.option_c: He sent him a royal decree
adults.option_d: He threatened him with punishment
adults.explanation: David made Uriah drunk, but Uriah went not down to his house; he lay at the door of the king's house (2 Samuel 11:13).

adults.question: What was Joab's tactical cover story to explain Uriah's death in the battle report to David?
adults.option_a: He said the enemy shot arrows from the wall and some of David's servants died
adults.option_b: He said Uriah deserted his post
adults.option_c: He said a plague broke out in the camp
adults.option_d: He said Uriah fell in a skirmish outside the city gates
adults.explanation: Joab instructed the messenger to say, "The men prevailed against us...thy servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also" (2 Samuel 11:23-24).

adults.question: How did David respond when the messenger reported Uriah's death?
adults.option_a: He said the sword devoureth one as well as another and told Joab to make his battle stronger
adults.option_b: He wept and tore his garments
adults.option_c: He gave Joab a great reward
adults.option_d: He was silent and sent the messenger away
adults.explanation: David told the messenger, "The sword devoureth one as well as another: make thy battle more strong against the city" (2 Samuel 11:25).

adults.question: What is the verdict the narrator gives at the very end of chapter 11?
adults.option_a: The thing that David had done displeased the LORD
adults.option_b: The LORD had mercy on David because of his repentance
adults.option_c: Joab bore the greater guilt before God
adults.option_d: David's sin was covered by his kingship
adults.explanation: But the thing that David had done displeased the LORD (2 Samuel 11:27).

adults.question: What was the full name of the woman David saw, according to the inquiry David made?
adults.option_a: Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite
adults.option_b: Bathsheba, the daughter of Ahithophel
adults.option_c: Bathsheba, the granddaughter of Ahithophel of Giloh
adults.option_d: Bathsheba, the daughter of Shimei the Benjamite
adults.explanation: One said, "Is not this Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite?" (2 Samuel 11:3).

adults.question: What does Uriah's loyalty in 2 Samuel 11 contrast against?
adults.option_a: David's adultery and murderous scheme while Uriah remained faithful to the covenant of war
adults.option_b: Joab's disloyalty to Uriah as a fellow soldier
adults.option_c: The cowardice of the other soldiers who fled the battle
adults.option_d: Nathan's silence in the face of injustice
adults.explanation: While David connived to cover his sin by having Uriah killed, Uriah refused even to go to his own home, saying the ark and Israel abide in tents (2 Samuel 11:11), highlighting David's moral failure by contrast.

kids.question: Who did God send to speak to David about his sin?
kids.explanation: The LORD sent Nathan unto David (2 Samuel 12:1).

kids.question: In Nathan's story, what did the rich man take from the poor man?
kids.explanation: The rich man took the poor man's little ewe lamb that he had bought and nourished (2 Samuel 12:4).

kids.question: What was the name of the son born to David and Bathsheba who became very wise?
kids.explanation: Bathsheba bare a son, and he called his name Solomon: and the LORD loved him (2 Samuel 12:24).

teens.question: What were Nathan's famous words to David after telling the parable of the ewe lamb?
teens.option_a: "Thou art the man"
teens.option_b: "God is watching you"
teens.option_c: "Repent or be destroyed"
teens.option_d: "The poor man cries to heaven"
teens.explanation: Nathan said to David, "Thou art the man" (2 Samuel 12:7).

teens.question: What consequences did God announce to David through Nathan?
teens.option_a: The sword shall never depart from his house, and evil would rise against him from his own house
teens.option_b: David would be killed immediately
teens.option_c: His kingdom would be given to his enemy Saul's line
teens.option_d: His army would be defeated in battle
teens.explanation: God said, "The sword shall never depart from thine house...I will raise up evil against thee out of thine own house" (2 Samuel 12:10-11).

teens.question: What happened to the child born of David and Bathsheba's sin?
teens.option_a: He became sick and died on the seventh day
teens.option_b: He grew up to become a judge
teens.option_c: He was taken away and raised by Nathan
teens.option_d: He was given to the priests
teens.explanation: The LORD struck the child that Uriah's wife bare unto David, and it was very sick...and on the seventh day the child died (2 Samuel 12:15, 18).

teens.question: What did David do when the child was sick that surprised his servants?
teens.option_a: He lay upon the earth and fasted and wept all night
teens.option_b: He offered sacrifices at the altar continually
teens.option_c: He sent the child to the best healers in the land
teens.option_d: He put on sackcloth and sat in the city gate
teens.explanation: David lay all night upon the earth, and the elders of his house could not persuade him to eat bread (2 Samuel 12:16-17).

teens.question: What city did Joab send word to David to come and take credit for capturing?
teens.option_a: Rabbah
teens.option_b: Jericho
teens.option_c: Gaza
teens.option_d: Ashkelon
teens.explanation: Joab sent messengers to David saying, "I have fought against Rabbah, and have taken the city of waters...come, and take the rest of the city" (2 Samuel 12:27-28).

adults.question: What did God say He had given David, listed by Nathan in the indictment?
adults.option_a: The house of Israel and Judah, Saul's house, Saul's wives, and would have given even more
adults.option_b: Only the kingdom of Judah
adults.option_c: The tabernacle and the ark of the covenant
adults.option_d: Victory in all his battles
adults.explanation: God said through Nathan, "I gave thee thy master's house...and gave thee the house of Israel and of Judah; and if that had been too little, I would moreover have given unto thee such and such things" (2 Samuel 12:8).

adults.question: What specific public consequence did God pronounce because David had done his deed in secret?
adults.option_a: His wives would be taken before all Israel by his neighbor in broad daylight
adults.option_b: His armies would be defeated publicly
adults.option_c: His name would be cursed in the streets
adults.option_d: His sons would be disinherited publicly
adults.explanation: God said, "I will take thy wives before thine eyes, and give them unto thy neighbour...for thou didst it secretly: but I will do this thing before all Israel, and before the sun" (2 Samuel 12:11-12).

adults.question: What did David say immediately when Nathan declared, "Thou art the man"?
adults.option_a: "I have sinned against the LORD"
adults.option_b: "Have mercy on me, O God"
adults.option_c: "Let Nathan be my witness"
adults.option_d: "What can I do to make it right?"
adults.explanation: David said unto Nathan, "I have sinned against the LORD" (2 Samuel 12:13).

adults.question: What was Nathan's immediate response to David's confession?
adults.option_a: "The LORD also hath put away thy sin; thou shalt not die"
adults.option_b: "Thou must offer a sacrifice for thy sin"
adults.option_c: "The people must know what thou hast done"
adults.option_d: "Thou must fast forty days"
adults.explanation: Nathan said unto David, "The LORD also hath put away thy sin; thou shalt not die" (2 Samuel 12:13).

adults.question: How did David's behavior change after the child actually died, surprising his servants?
adults.option_a: He arose from the earth, washed, anointed himself, changed his apparel, worshipped, and then ate
adults.option_b: He increased his mourning and refused food for another seven days
adults.option_c: He went immediately to battle to distract his grief
adults.option_d: He called Nathan back to interpret God's will
adults.explanation: When the child was dead, David arose from the earth, washed, anointed himself, changed his apparel, went into the house of the LORD and worshipped, then ate bread (2 Samuel 12:20).

adults.question: What reason did David give for ceasing to mourn after the child died?
adults.option_a: "Can I bring him back again? I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me"
adults.option_b: "God has taken him as a sign of forgiveness"
adults.option_c: "The child is now with Samuel in paradise"
adults.option_d: "Further mourning displeases the LORD"
adults.explanation: David said, "Can I bring him back again? I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me" (2 Samuel 12:23).

adults.question: What other name did God, through Nathan, give to Solomon at birth?
adults.option_a: Jedidiah, meaning beloved of the LORD
adults.option_b: Emmanuel, meaning God with us
adults.option_c: Josiah, meaning healed by God
adults.option_d: Elijah, meaning my God is Yahweh
adults.explanation: The LORD loved Solomon and sent Nathan the prophet, who called his name Jedidiah, because of the LORD (2 Samuel 12:24-25).

adults.question: What crown did David take from the king of Rabbah, and what was its weight?
adults.option_a: The crown of Milcom weighing a talent of gold, with precious stones set on David's head
adults.option_b: A silver crown weighing fifty shekels
adults.option_c: An iron crown weighing a hundred talents
adults.option_d: A golden crown weighing two talents
adults.explanation: David took their king's crown...the weight whereof was a talent of gold with the precious stones: and it was set on David's head (2 Samuel 12:30).

kids.question: Who was Tamar's brother who harmed her?
kids.explanation: Amnon the son of David loved Tamar, the sister of Absalom the son of David (2 Samuel 13:1).

kids.question: What did Absalom do to Amnon because of what Amnon did to Tamar?
kids.option_b: He sent him away from the palace
kids.explanation: Absalom commanded his servants to smite Amnon, and all the king's sons arose and fled (2 Samuel 13:28-29).

kids.question: Where did Absalom flee after killing Amnon?
kids.explanation: Absalom fled, and went to Geshur, and was there three years (2 Samuel 13:37-38).

teens.question: Who was Jonadab, and what did he advise Amnon to do?
teens.option_a: He was Amnon's cousin, a very subtle man, who advised Amnon to feign illness so Tamar would come to him
teens.option_b: He was Amnon's armor-bearer who advised him to flee the country
teens.option_c: He was a palace official who warned Amnon to confess to David
teens.option_d: He was a priest who counseled Amnon to offer a sacrifice
teens.explanation: Jonadab was the son of Shimeah, David's brother, a very subtil man, who advised Amnon to feign sickness and ask David to send Tamar to make him food (2 Samuel 13:3-5).

teens.question: What did Tamar say to Amnon before he assaulted her, pleading with him?
teens.option_a: She said, "No such thing ought to be done in Israel: speak to the king; for he will not withhold me from thee"
teens.option_b: She said, "I will tell our father David what you are planning"
teens.option_c: She said, "God will punish you for this wickedness"
teens.option_d: She said, "Let me go and I will keep silent about this"
teens.explanation: Tamar said, "Nay, my brother, do not force me...speak unto the king; for he will not withhold me from thee" (2 Samuel 13:12-13).

teens.question: How did Amnon feel toward Tamar after he had committed his sin against her?
teens.option_a: He hated her exceedingly, and the hatred was greater than the love with which he had loved her
teens.option_b: He was filled with remorse and begged her forgiveness
teens.option_c: He wanted to make her his wife
teens.option_d: He ignored her completely
teens.explanation: Then Amnon hated her exceedingly; so that the hatred wherewith he hated her was greater than the love wherewith he had loved her (2 Samuel 13:15).

teens.question: What was Absalom's attitude toward Amnon in the days following the assault on Tamar?
teens.option_a: Absalom spake unto his brother Amnon neither good nor bad, for Absalom hated Amnon
teens.option_b: Absalom immediately confronted Amnon and demanded justice
teens.option_c: Absalom pretended to forgive Amnon publicly
teens.option_d: Absalom avoided Jerusalem so as not to see Amnon
teens.explanation: Absalom spake unto his brother Amnon neither good nor bad: for Absalom hated Amnon, because he had forced his sister Tamar (2 Samuel 13:22).

teens.question: How long did Absalom dwell in Geshur before David's soul longed to go to Absalom?
teens.option_a: Three years
teens.option_b: Seven years
teens.option_c: One year
teens.option_d: Five years
teens.explanation: So Absalom fled, and went to Geshur, and was there three years (2 Samuel 13:38).

adults.question: What was the plan Absalom used to gather all the king's sons so Amnon would be among them?
adults.option_a: He invited all the king's sons to a sheepshearing feast and asked the king to send Amnon specifically
adults.option_b: He arranged a royal banquet at the palace
adults.option_c: He organized a hunting expedition in the hills of Ephraim
adults.option_d: He held a sacrifice and invited the royal family
adults.explanation: Absalom had sheepshearers in Baal-hazor, and invited all the king's sons; then he asked the king, "Let Amnon go with us" (2 Samuel 13:23-27).

adults.question: What instruction did Absalom give his servants regarding the timing of killing Amnon?
adults.option_a: "When Amnon's heart is merry with wine, then kill him"
adults.option_b: "Strike him when he is alone and away from the others"
adults.option_c: "Wait until he sleeps and then smite him"
adults.option_d: "Kill him only if he refuses to leave the feast"
adults.explanation: Absalom commanded his servants, "Mark ye now when Amnon's heart is merry with wine, and when I say unto you, Smite Amnon; then kill him, fear not" (2 Samuel 13:28).

adults.question: What false report initially reached David about the massacre at Absalom's feast?
adults.option_a: That Absalom had slain all the king's sons
adults.option_b: That Amnon alone had started a riot and been killed accidentally
adults.option_c: That the Philistines had attacked the feast
adults.option_d: That Joab had killed Amnon by royal command
adults.explanation: A messenger told David, "Absalom hath slain all the king's sons, and there is not one of them left" (2 Samuel 13:30).

adults.question: Who corrected the false report and what was the truth he told David?
adults.option_a: Jonadab corrected it, saying only Amnon was dead, as Absalom had determined this since the day Amnon forced Tamar
adults.option_b: Nathan the prophet corrected it, declaring it was God's judgment
adults.option_c: Joab corrected it, having received information from spies
adults.option_d: The returning servants corrected it when they arrived
adults.explanation: Jonadab said to David, "Let not my lord suppose that they have slain all the young men the king's sons; for Amnon only is dead: for by the appointment of Absalom this hath been determined from the day that he forced his sister Tamar" (2 Samuel 13:32-33).

adults.question: Where had Tamar lived after the assault, and what was her condition?
adults.option_a: She lived desolate in her brother Absalom's house
adults.option_b: She was taken into the king's harem for protection
adults.option_c: She returned to her mother's household
adults.option_d: She was sent to live in the tabernacle precincts
adults.explanation: Tamar remained desolate in her brother Absalom's house (2 Samuel 13:20).

adults.question: What was David's reaction when he heard of Amnon's assault on Tamar?
adults.option_a: He was very wroth but did not grieve or punish Amnon because he loved him
adults.option_b: He immediately punished Amnon and stripped him of his inheritance
adults.option_c: He banished Amnon from Jerusalem for a year
adults.option_d: He called for a public trial before the elders
adults.explanation: When King David heard of all these things, he was very wroth (2 Samuel 13:21), yet he did not punish Amnon because he loved him as his firstborn.

adults.question: Who was Absalom's grandfather in Geshur, where he fled?
adults.option_a: Talmai, king of Geshur
adults.option_b: Achish, king of Gath
adults.option_c: Nahash, king of Ammon
adults.option_d: Hadadezer, king of Zobah
adults.explanation: Absalom fled, and went to Talmai, the son of Ammihud, king of Geshur (2 Samuel 13:37).

adults.question: What does chapter 13 reveal as the beginning of the fulfillment of Nathan's prophecy in chapter 12?
adults.option_a: It shows "the sword shall never depart from thine house" beginning through rape and fratricide within David's own family
adults.option_b: It shows God's blessing transferred from David to Solomon immediately
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that Joab's influence corrupted the royal household
adults.option_d: It reveals that Saul's curse on David's line came to pass
adults.explanation: Nathan had warned that evil would arise from David's own house (2 Samuel 12:11); the violation of Tamar and murder of Amnon show that prophecy beginning to be fulfilled (2 Samuel 13:14, 28-29).

kids.question: From what city did Joab bring a wise woman to help bring Absalom back?
kids.explanation: Joab sent to Tekoa, and fetched thence a wise woman (2 Samuel 14:2).

kids.question: How many years was Absalom known for having no blemish and being very handsome?
kids.option_a: He was praised for his beauty in all Israel with no blemish from head to foot
kids.option_b: He was praised only in Judah
kids.option_c: Only his hair was mentioned as beautiful
kids.option_d: He was not described as handsome
kids.explanation: In all Israel there was none to be so much praised as Absalom for his beauty: from the sole of his foot even to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him (2 Samuel 14:25).

kids.question: How long was Absalom in Jerusalem before King David finally agreed to see him?
kids.option_a: Two full years before Absalom sent for Joab, then David kissed him
kids.explanation: Absalom dwelt two full years in Jerusalem, and saw not the king's face (2 Samuel 14:28), then Joab arranged the reconciliation and David kissed Absalom (14:33).

teens.question: What was the fictional story the wise woman of Tekoa told David?
teens.option_a: She said she was a widow whose two sons fought, one killing the other, and the family wanted to execute the surviving son
teens.option_b: She said her husband had been killed in battle and she was left destitute
teens.option_c: She said her son had been falsely accused and condemned to death
teens.option_d: She said two brothers fought over an inheritance and one was exiled
teens.explanation: The woman pretended to be a widow who had two sons; one smote the other and slew him, and the whole family sought to kill the survivor (2 Samuel 14:5-7).

teens.question: What point did the woman of Tekoa make that applied to David and Absalom?
teens.option_a: She said the king had not fetched home again his banished, and used the figure of water spilt on the ground that cannot be gathered up
teens.option_b: She said that vengeance belongs only to the LORD
teens.option_c: She argued that exile was worse than death
teens.option_d: She compared Absalom to a lost sheep that needed finding
teens.explanation: The woman said, "For we must needs die, and are as water spilt on the ground...neither doth God respect any person: yet doth he devise means, that his banished be not expelled from him" (2 Samuel 14:14).

teens.question: How did David discern that Joab was behind the wise woman's visit?
teens.option_a: David perceived that Joab had sent her, and she confessed that Joab had put all the words in her mouth
teens.option_b: A servant overheard Joab giving her instructions
teens.option_c: Joab himself came forward and admitted it
teens.option_d: The woman's story was too well-crafted for a simple widow
teens.explanation: David said, "Is not the hand of Joab with thee in all this?" and the woman acknowledged that Joab had commanded her and put all those words in her mouth (2 Samuel 14:19).

teens.question: How did Absalom get Joab to come to him after Joab refused to visit him twice?
teens.option_a: He set Joab's barley field on fire
teens.option_b: He sent a threatening letter to Joab
teens.option_c: He appealed to the king through a servant
teens.option_d: He blocked the road to the palace
teens.explanation: Absalom said to his servants, "Set Joab's field on fire." So his servants set the field on fire, and then Joab came to Absalom's house (2 Samuel 14:30-31).

teens.question: What was the weight of Absalom's hair when he cut it at the end of each year?
teens.option_a: Two hundred shekels after the king's weight
teens.option_b: One hundred shekels
teens.option_c: Fifty shekels
teens.option_d: Three hundred shekels
teens.explanation: When he polled his head...he weighed the hair of his head at two hundred shekels after the king's weight (2 Samuel 14:26).

adults.question: Why did Joab devise the plan to bring Absalom back to Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: Because Joab perceived that the king's heart was toward Absalom
adults.option_b: Because Absalom had bribed Joab with silver
adults.option_c: Because the army was loyal to Absalom and demanded his return
adults.option_d: Because Nathan the prophet instructed Joab
adults.explanation: Joab the son of Zeruiah perceived that the king's heart was toward Absalom (2 Samuel 14:1).

adults.question: What did the wise woman say to David comparing God's nature to the king's actions?
adults.option_a: She said God devises means that his banished be not expelled from him, implying David should do the same
adults.option_b: She said God always punishes the guilty without mercy
adults.option_c: She said God rewards the king who shows justice above mercy
adults.option_d: She said God told her in a vision to bring Absalom home
adults.explanation: The woman said, "God doth not take away life; but devises means, that his banished be not expelled from him" (2 Samuel 14:14), applying God's character as a model for David to recall Absalom.

adults.question: What exact terms did David grant when he recalled Absalom?
adults.option_a: Let Absalom turn to his own house and let him not see the king's face
adults.option_b: Full reconciliation with Absalom restored as crown prince
adults.option_c: Absalom could live in Jerusalem only outside the palace quarter
adults.option_d: Absalom had to serve five years in the army before being forgiven
adults.explanation: David said, "Let him turn to his own house, and let him not see my face." So Absalom returned to his own house, and saw not the king's face (2 Samuel 14:24).

adults.question: How many sons and one daughter did Absalom have, and what was his daughter's name?
adults.option_a: Three sons and one daughter named Tamar
adults.option_b: Two sons and one daughter named Abigail
adults.option_c: Four sons and two daughters
adults.option_d: One son and one daughter named Miriam
adults.explanation: Unto Absalom there were born three sons, and one daughter, whose name was Tamar: she was a woman of a fair countenance (2 Samuel 14:27).

adults.question: What argument did Absalom use with Joab when he finally arranged a meeting?
adults.option_a: He said it would have been better for him to have remained in Geshur, and asked Joab to arrange for him to see the king's face or else kill him
adults.option_b: He threatened to expose Joab's role in the field burning
adults.option_c: He offered Joab gold and silver to restore his standing
adults.option_d: He said the people demanded his full restoration
adults.explanation: Absalom said to Joab, "It had been good for me to have been there still: now therefore let me see the king's face; and if there be any iniquity in me, let him kill me" (2 Samuel 14:32).

adults.question: How did David greet Absalom when they were finally reconciled?
adults.option_a: The king kissed Absalom
adults.option_b: The king embraced Absalom and wept
adults.option_c: The king gave Absalom a seat at his table
adults.option_d: The king restored Absalom's rank publicly
adults.explanation: Absalom came to the king, and bowed himself on his face to the ground before the king: and the king kissed Absalom (2 Samuel 14:33).

adults.question: What does Absalom's burning of Joab's field reveal about his character?
adults.option_a: A ruthless willingness to use destructive means to force others to serve his agenda
adults.option_b: Frustration that his legitimate requests were being ignored
adults.option_c: A righteous demand for justice after years of unjust exile
adults.option_d: A calculated act meant to demonstrate his power to the people
adults.explanation: When Joab refused to come to him twice, Absalom told his servants to set Joab's barley field ablaze to compel Joab's attention (2 Samuel 14:30), revealing a pattern of coercion that foreshadows his later rebellion.

adults.question: What is the theological irony in Absalom's partial restoration described in chapter 14?
adults.option_a: David's incomplete forgiveness — recalling Absalom but refusing to see him — sowed the bitterness that grew into full rebellion in chapter 15
adults.option_b: God blessed David for his measured response to Absalom's sin
adults.option_c: Joab's intervention was the beginning of his own downfall
adults.option_d: Absalom's beauty was a sign of divine favor restoring him fully
adults.explanation: Though David allowed Absalom to return to Jerusalem, he refused to see him for two full years (2 Samuel 14:24, 28), a half-hearted reconciliation that left Absalom embittered and primed for the rebellion that follows in chapter 15.

kids.question: What did Absalom use to make himself look important each day?
kids.option_a: Chariots and horses and fifty men to run before him
kids.option_b: A golden crown and royal robes
kids.option_c: A large army of soldiers beside him
kids.option_d: Beautiful gifts for everyone he met
kids.explanation: Absalom prepared him chariots and horses, and fifty men to run before him (2 Samuel 15:1).

kids.question: What city did Absalom go to when he began his rebellion and was made king?
kids.explanation: Absalom arose, and went to Hebron (2 Samuel 15:9-10).

kids.question: What did David do when he heard that Absalom had taken over?
kids.option_a: He fled from Jerusalem with all his household
kids.option_b: He called his army together to fight
kids.option_d: He sent a messenger to Absalom
kids.explanation: David said to all his servants, "Arise, and let us flee...lest he overtake us suddenly." So David and all his household fled on foot (2 Samuel 15:14-16).

teens.question: How did Absalom steal the hearts of the men of Israel?
teens.option_a: He stood at the gate and intercepted men with disputes, saying the king had no deputy, and that he wished he were judge so he could do them justice
teens.option_b: He held great feasts and gave gifts to the people
teens.option_c: He gathered the army and promised them land
teens.option_d: He spoke against David in the temple courts
teens.explanation: Absalom would intercept men coming to the king for judgment and say, "There is no man deputed of the king to hear thee...O that I were made judge in the land...I would do him justice": so Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel (2 Samuel 15:2-6).

teens.question: What vow did Absalom claim he had made to get permission to go to Hebron?
teens.option_a: He said he had vowed a vow to the LORD in Geshur that if the LORD brought him to Jerusalem, he would serve the LORD in Hebron
teens.option_b: He said he had promised God a sacrifice if his rebellion succeeded
teens.option_c: He said he had vowed to offer a firstfruits offering in Hebron
teens.option_d: He said he had promised to visit his mother's grave in Hebron
teens.explanation: Absalom said to David, "I pray thee, let me go and pay my vow, which I have vowed unto the LORD, in Hebron" (2 Samuel 15:7-8).

teens.question: Who was Hushai the Archite and what mission did David give him?
teens.option_a: He was David's friend, sent back to Jerusalem to defeat Ahithophel's counsel and spy for David
teens.option_b: He was a military commander sent to defend Jerusalem
teens.option_c: He was a priest sent to keep the ark in Jerusalem
teens.option_d: He was a scribe sent to record the events of the rebellion
teens.explanation: Hushai the Archite, David's friend, came to David, and David sent him back saying, "Return to the city...defeat the counsel of Ahithophel" and report to David through the priests (2 Samuel 15:32-36).

teens.question: What did David ask the priests Zadok and Abiathar to do when they brought the ark out of Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: He asked them to carry the ark back into the city and remain there as spies
teens.option_b: He asked them to bring the ark with the army to fight Absalom
teens.option_c: He asked them to hide the ark in a safe place outside the city
teens.option_d: He asked them to offer sacrifices at the Mount of Olives
teens.explanation: David told Zadok, "Carry back the ark of God into the city...thou art a seer: return into the city in peace, and your two sons with you, Ahimaaz and Jonathan" to serve as messengers (2 Samuel 15:25-29).

teens.question: How did David go up the Mount of Olives as he fled Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: He went up barefoot and weeping, with his head covered, and all the people with him covered their heads and wept
teens.option_b: He rode a horse and ordered silence
teens.option_c: He walked alone ahead of all his servants
teens.option_d: He was carried in a litter because of grief
teens.explanation: David went up by the ascent of mount Olivet, and wept as he went up, and had his head covered, and he went barefoot: and all the people...every man with his head covered wept as they went up (2 Samuel 15:30).

adults.question: How long did Absalom's campaign to steal the hearts of Israel last before he felt confident to rebel?
adults.option_a: Forty years
adults.option_b: Four years
adults.option_c: Ten years
adults.option_d: Seven years
adults.explanation: After forty years Absalom said to the king, "I pray thee, let me go and pay my vow" — note some manuscripts read four years, but the Hebrew text says forty years (2 Samuel 15:7).

adults.question: What was the significance of Absalom choosing Hebron as the site of his coronation?
adults.option_a: Hebron was the site of the tabernacle and the ark of God
adults.option_b: Hebron was David's original capital where he had first been made king over Judah, giving Absalom's coup symbolic legitimacy
adults.option_c: Hebron was Absalom's birthplace
adults.option_d: Hebron was near the Philistine border giving Absalom military advantage
adults.explanation: David had reigned in Hebron seven years before Jerusalem (2 Samuel 2:11, 5:5); Absalom's choosing Hebron as his coronation site (2 Samuel 15:10) exploited that symbolic capital.

adults.question: Who was Ahithophel and why was his involvement in Absalom's rebellion particularly significant to David?
adults.option_a: Ahithophel was David's own counselor, and his defection was so grievous David prayed God would turn his counsel into foolishness
adults.option_b: Ahithophel was the head of the Sanhedrin whose support gave the rebellion legal authority
adults.option_c: Ahithophel was a Philistine diplomat whose alliance strengthened Absalom militarily
adults.option_d: Ahithophel was Joab's uncle who gave Absalom military expertise
adults.explanation: David was told, "Ahithophel is among the conspirators with Absalom"; David said, "O LORD, I pray thee, turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness" (2 Samuel 15:31).

adults.question: How many men did Absalom invite from Jerusalem under false pretenses to go to Hebron with him?
adults.option_a: Two hundred men who went in their simplicity and knew not any thing
adults.option_b: Five hundred loyal soldiers
adults.option_c: Twelve tribal elders
adults.option_d: A thousand men of valor
adults.explanation: With Absalom went two hundred men out of Jerusalem...and they went in their simplicity, and knew not any thing (2 Samuel 15:11).

adults.question: What did Ittai the Gittite say when David urged him to turn back rather than flee with him?
adults.option_a: He swore by the LORD that as David lived, whether in death or life, he would not leave David
adults.option_b: He said he would fight for David until his last breath
adults.option_c: He said God had spoken to him in a dream to stay with David
adults.option_d: He said his soldiers needed David's leadership to survive
adults.explanation: Ittai answered, "As the LORD liveth, and as my lord the king liveth, surely in what place my lord the king shall be, whether in death or life, even there also will thy servant be" (2 Samuel 15:21).

adults.question: What was the intelligence network David set up before leaving Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: Zadok and Abiathar with the ark inside Jerusalem, their sons as runners, and Hushai to counter Ahithophel's counsel and report through the priests
adults.option_b: Joab left behind with a hidden garrison in the city
adults.option_c: Mephibosheth tasked with observing Absalom's movements
adults.option_d: A network of merchants traveling between the armies
adults.explanation: David arranged for Zadok and Abiathar to stay in Jerusalem with the ark, their sons Ahimaaz and Jonathan as messengers, and Hushai to defeat Ahithophel's counsel (2 Samuel 15:27-36).

adults.question: What does Psalm 3 tradition associate with David's flight in this chapter?
adults.option_a: Psalm 3 is titled "A Psalm of David, when he fled from Absalom his son," beginning "LORD, how are they increased that trouble me"
adults.option_b: Psalm 23 was composed during the flight across the Jordan
adults.option_c: Psalm 51 was written at the Mount of Olives as David wept
adults.option_d: Psalm 46 was David's prayer while crossing the Jordan River
adults.explanation: The superscription of Psalm 3 reads, "A Psalm of David, when he fled from Absalom his son," connecting it to this flight over the Mount of Olives (2 Samuel 15:30).

adults.question: What does David's statement about the ark in 2 Samuel 15:25-26 reveal about his theology of God's sovereignty?
adults.option_a: He says if the LORD has delight in him He will bring him again, but if not, "here I am, let him do to me as seemeth good unto him," showing submission to God's will
adults.option_b: He declares the ark must go with the army or Israel will lose the battle
adults.option_c: He believes God's presence guarantees his return to the throne
adults.option_d: He uses the ark as a political symbol to maintain priestly loyalty
adults.explanation: David said, "Carry back the ark of God into the city: if I shall find favour in the eyes of the LORD, he will bring me again...but if he thus say, I have no delight in thee; behold, here am I, let him do to me as seemeth good unto him" (2 Samuel 15:25-26).

kids.question: Who met David with donkeys loaded with food as he fled Jerusalem?
kids.explanation: Ziba the servant of Mephibosheth met David with a couple of asses saddled, with bread, summer fruits, and wine (2 Samuel 16:1).

kids.question: Who cursed David with stones and dirt as David and his men passed by?
kids.explanation: Shimei the son of Gera...came forth, and cursed still as he came, and he threw stones at David (2 Samuel 16:5, 13).

kids.question: Who came to Jerusalem and met Absalom, pretending to be on Absalom's side, when really he was David's spy?
kids.explanation: Hushai the Archite, David's friend, came unto Absalom: and Absalom said unto Hushai, "Is this thy kindness to thy friend?" (2 Samuel 16:16-17).

teens.question: What did Ziba claim about Mephibosheth when he brought David provisions?
teens.option_a: He said Mephibosheth was staying in Jerusalem saying, "To day shall the house of Israel restore me the kingdom of my father"
teens.option_b: He said Mephibosheth had fled to Geshur to be safe
teens.option_c: He said Mephibosheth was too ill to travel
teens.option_d: He said Mephibosheth had refused to help either side
teens.explanation: Ziba answered, "Behold, he abideth at Jerusalem: for he said, To day shall the house of Israel restore me the kingdom of my father Saul" (2 Samuel 16:3).

teens.question: What did David give Ziba as a reward for his provision?
teens.option_a: All that pertained to Mephibosheth
teens.option_b: A hundred talents of silver
teens.option_c: A village and its lands
teens.option_d: The position of royal steward
teens.explanation: The king said to Ziba, "Behold, thine are all that pertained unto Mephibosheth" (2 Samuel 16:4).

teens.question: From what tribe was Shimei, and why was he cursing David?
teens.option_a: He was of the family of Saul's house of the tribe of Benjamin, and he cursed David saying God had given the kingdom to Absalom because of David's blood guiltiness
teens.option_b: He was of the tribe of Reuben, cursing David for taxing the northern tribes
teens.option_c: He was of the tribe of Dan, cursing David for moving the ark to Jerusalem
teens.option_d: He was of the tribe of Gad, cursing David for failing to avenge Abner
teens.explanation: Shimei the son of Gera, a Benjamite of the house of Saul, came forth and cursed, saying, "The LORD hath returned upon thee all the blood of the house of Saul...the LORD hath delivered the kingdom into the hand of Absalom" (2 Samuel 16:5, 8).

teens.question: What did David say when Abishai offered to go cut off Shimei's head for cursing the king?
teens.option_a: He said let him curse, for the LORD had bidden him to curse David, and perhaps the LORD would look on his affliction
teens.option_b: He said, "Bring him to me so I may judge him myself"
teens.option_c: He said, "Do what you must to protect the king's honor"
teens.option_d: He said, "Not today, but he shall answer for this later"
teens.explanation: David said, "Let him curse, because the LORD hath said unto him, Curse David...Let him alone, and let him curse; for the LORD hath bidden him...It may be that the LORD will look on mine affliction" (2 Samuel 16:10-12).

teens.question: What did Absalom do in Jerusalem on the advice of Ahithophel that fulfilled Nathan's prophecy?
teens.option_a: He went in unto his father's concubines in a tent on the roof before all Israel
teens.option_b: He sat on David's throne and made sacrifices at the altar
teens.option_c: He took possession of the royal treasury
teens.option_d: He held a public coronation ceremony in the palace courtyard
teens.explanation: Ahithophel counseled Absalom to go in unto his father's concubines; they spread Absalom a tent upon the top of the house, and Absalom went in unto his father's concubines in the sight of all Israel (2 Samuel 16:21-22), fulfilling 2 Samuel 12:11-12.

adults.question: What is the specific purpose Ahithophel gave Absalom for taking his father's concubines?
adults.option_a: So that all Israel shall hear that thou art abhorred of thy father: then shall the hands of all that are with thee be strong
adults.option_b: To demonstrate that Absalom had fully claimed the throne
adults.option_c: To punish the concubines for their loyalty to David
adults.option_d: To fulfill a political custom of the ancient Near East
adults.explanation: Ahithophel said, "Go in unto thy father's concubines...and all Israel shall hear that thou art abhorred of thy father: then shall the hands of all that are with thee be strong" (2 Samuel 16:21).

adults.question: How is Ahithophel's counsel described in verse 23 of chapter 16?
adults.option_a: The counsel of Ahithophel was as if a man had enquired at the oracle of God
adults.option_b: Ahithophel's counsel was sharper than a two-edged sword
adults.option_c: Ahithophel was the wisest man in all the eastern kingdoms
adults.option_d: His words were as fire and carried the authority of a prophet
adults.explanation: The counsel of Ahithophel, which he counselled in those days, was as if a man had enquired at the oracle of God: so was all the counsel of Ahithophel both with David and with Absalom (2 Samuel 16:23).

adults.question: What greeting did Hushai give Absalom when he arrived in Jerusalem, and how did Hushai justify it?
adults.option_a: He cried "God save the king!" and when questioned he said he would serve whoever the LORD and the people chose
adults.option_b: He bowed down before Absalom and offered him military counsel
adults.option_c: He said he had come to tell Absalom of weaknesses in David's defenses
adults.option_d: He presented a gift of silver to demonstrate his loyalty to Absalom
adults.explanation: Hushai said to Absalom, "God save the king," and when Absalom questioned his loyalty to his friend David, Hushai said, "Nay; but whom the LORD, and this people, and all the men of Israel, choose, his will I be" (2 Samuel 16:16-18).

adults.question: What does Ziba's self-interested report about Mephibosheth in chapter 16 set up for chapter 19?
adults.option_a: It sets up the reversal where Mephibosheth gives his own account to David and David has to divide the land between them
adults.option_b: It establishes Ziba as the new steward over all of Saul's estate permanently
adults.option_c: It shows David's generosity in rewarding loyalty during his flight
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that loyalty to the Saulide line had permanently ended
adults.explanation: Ziba's false accusation that Mephibosheth expected to reclaim Saul's kingdom (2 Samuel 16:3) is later contradicted by Mephibosheth himself (2 Samuel 19:24-28), exposing Ziba as a liar; David splits the estate between them (19:29).

adults.question: What theological principle did David articulate in his response to Shimei's cursing?
adults.option_a: That even the cursing of an enemy may be permitted by God as chastisement, and should be endured with humility rather than violent retaliation
adults.option_b: That kings are immune from curses and Shimei's words had no power
adults.option_c: That to retaliate against curses compounds the original sin
adults.option_d: That revenge belongs to the king and should be delayed until victory
adults.explanation: David said, "Let him curse, because the LORD hath said unto him, Curse David...the LORD hath bidden him...It may be that the LORD will look on mine affliction, and that the LORD will requite me good for his cursing this day" (2 Samuel 16:10-12).

adults.question: Why did Absalom question Hushai's loyalty when he arrived in Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: He asked why Hushai had not gone with his friend David when David fled
adults.option_b: He suspected Hushai of being a spy based on a report from Ahithophel
adults.option_c: He knew Hushai had served David as a counselor for many years
adults.option_d: He received a report that Hushai had been seen consulting with the priests
adults.explanation: Absalom said to Hushai, "Is this thy kindness to thy friend? why wentest thou not with thy friend?" (2 Samuel 16:17).

adults.question: What supplies did Ziba bring to David and his men?
adults.option_a: Two hundred loaves of bread, an hundred bunches of raisins, an hundred of summer fruits, and a bottle of wine
adults.option_b: A cart of grain and two oxen for sacrifice
adults.option_c: Gold and silver for David's military expenses
adults.option_d: Weapons and armor for David's soldiers
adults.explanation: Ziba had two hundred loaves of bread, and an hundred bunches of raisins, and an hundred of summer fruits, and a bottle of wine (2 Samuel 16:1).

adults.question: What does the detail that Absalom's tent was pitched on the very roof of the house indicate about the fulfillment of Nathan's prophecy?
adults.option_a: It mirrors the exact setting of David's own sin — David had seen Bathsheba from his rooftop — and now Absalom's sin on the same rooftop was done publicly, fulfilling "before the sun"
adults.option_b: It shows Absalom wanted to demonstrate his military control of the palace heights
adults.option_c: It fulfilled the ancient custom of a new king claiming the highest point of the palace
adults.option_d: It indicated that Absalom feared an assassination attempt inside the palace
adults.explanation: God had said through Nathan, "I will take thy wives...and he shall lie with thy wives in the sight of this sun" (2 Samuel 12:11-12); David had stood on his roof to see Bathsheba (11:2), and now Absalom committed his sin on that very rooftop "in the sight of all Israel, and before the sun" (16:22).

kids.question: Whose advice did Absalom follow — Ahithophel's or Hushai's?
kids.explanation: Absalom and all the men of Israel said, "The counsel of Hushai the Archite is better than the counsel of Ahithophel" (2 Samuel 17:14).

kids.question: What did Ahithophel do when he saw his advice was not followed?
kids.option_a: He saddled his donkey, went home, set his house in order, and hanged himself
kids.option_d: He threw himself on the mercy of the king
kids.explanation: When Ahithophel saw that his counsel was not followed, he saddled his ass, arose, gat him home to his house...and hanged himself (2 Samuel 17:23).

kids.question: Where did David cross to escape Absalom's army?
kids.option_b: He hid in a cave in the wilderness
kids.option_c: He went to the Philistine city of Gath
kids.option_d: He sailed across the Sea of Galilee
kids.explanation: David arose, and all the people that were with him, and they passed over Jordan (2 Samuel 17:22).

teens.question: What was Ahithophel's specific counsel to Absalom?
teens.option_a: Let me choose twelve thousand men and arise and pursue David this night and smite him alone while he is weary and weak-handed
teens.option_b: Build a strong defense of Jerusalem and wait for David to surrender
teens.option_c: Make alliances with the Philistines and surround David from all sides
teens.option_d: Call all Israel together before attacking to ensure maximum force
teens.explanation: Ahithophel counseled, "Let me now choose out twelve thousand men, and I will arise and pursue after David this night: And I will come upon him while he is weary and weak handed...I will smite the king only" (2 Samuel 17:1-2).

teens.question: What was Hushai's counter-counsel that convinced Absalom?
teens.option_a: He said David and his men are mighty men and Ahithophel's plan would fail; gather all Israel from Dan to Beersheba and lead them personally
teens.option_b: He said Ahithophel's plan was good but the timing was wrong
teens.option_c: He counseled Absalom to send diplomats to David first
teens.option_d: He suggested surrounding the Jordan crossings to trap David
teens.explanation: Hushai said Ahithophel's counsel was not good, for David's men were mighty; instead "I counsel that all Israel be generally gathered unto thee...and thou go to battle in thine own person" (2 Samuel 17:7-11).

teens.question: Why did the LORD arrange for Absalom to follow Hushai's counsel instead of Ahithophel's?
teens.option_a: Because the LORD had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel to bring evil upon Absalom
teens.option_b: Because Hushai was more persuasive than Ahithophel
teens.option_c: Because the elders of Israel voted for Hushai's plan
teens.option_d: Because Absalom's generals preferred Hushai's larger strategy
teens.explanation: The LORD had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel, to the intent that the LORD might bring evil upon Absalom (2 Samuel 17:14).

teens.question: How did the message from Hushai reach David — who were the messengers?
teens.option_a: A wench told Ahimaaz and Jonathan who hid in a well at Bahurim, then fled to David
teens.option_b: Hushai wrote a letter hidden in bread sent across the Jordan
teens.option_c: Zadok the priest carried the message himself by night
teens.option_d: Two Levites disguised as merchants carried the message
teens.explanation: A woman told Ahimaaz and Jonathan (who hid in a well at Bahurim when spotted), and they went and told king David (2 Samuel 17:17-21).

teens.question: Who sheltered Ahimaaz and Jonathan when Absalom's servant searched for them?
teens.option_a: A woman of Bahurim who hid them in a well and covered it with ground corn
teens.option_b: A Levite who hid them in the tabernacle storeroom
teens.option_c: A widow in the Jordan valley who disguised them as farmhands
teens.option_d: The priests of Nob who sheltered them in the sanctuary
teens.explanation: There was a well in the court of a man of Bahurim; a woman spread a covering over the well's mouth and spread ground corn on it, so Absalom's servants found them not (2 Samuel 17:18-20).

adults.question: What was the strategic genius of Ahithophel's plan that Hushai acknowledged in order to refute it?
adults.option_a: It would kill David alone quickly before his forces could regroup, preventing a prolonged civil war and saving lives on both sides
adults.option_b: It minimized the risk of defections to David from the army
adults.option_c: It used the terrain of the Jordan valley to David's disadvantage
adults.option_d: It would cut off David's supply lines before he could cross the Jordan
adults.explanation: Ahithophel's plan was to strike while David was weary and weak-handed, kill the king only, and bring all the people back as a bride returns to her husband (2 Samuel 17:2-3). Hushai acknowledged David's mighty men and the danger of failure (17:8-10) to undercut confidence in the swift plan.

adults.question: What memorable analogy did Hushai use to describe how Israel would fall upon David under his plan?
adults.option_a: As the dew falleth on the ground, so shall we come upon him in one of the places where he shall be found
adults.option_b: As a lion pounces on its prey in the night
adults.option_c: As the flood overtakes the valley
adults.option_d: As the eagle swoops down from the mountain
adults.explanation: Hushai said, "We will light upon him as the dew falleth on the ground: and of him and of all the men that are with him there shall not be left so much as one" (2 Samuel 17:12).

adults.question: Who was Shobi the son of Nahash and what did he bring to David at Mahanaim?
adults.option_a: He was the son of the king of Ammon who brought beds, basins, earthen vessels, wheat, barley, flour, parched corn, beans, lentils, honey, butter, sheep, and cheese to David
adults.option_b: He was a Philistine commander who defected to David with weapons
adults.option_c: He was a Gileadite elder who supplied David with soldiers
adults.option_d: He was a Levite who brought sacred offerings for David's troops
adults.explanation: Shobi the son of Nahash of Rabbah of Ammon, Machir the son of Ammiel of Lo-debar, and Barzillai brought beds, basins, earthen vessels, wheat, barley, flour, parched corn, beans, lentils, honey, butter, sheep, and cheese of kine for David and his people (2 Samuel 17:27-29).

adults.question: What does the suicide of Ahithophel theologically parallel in the New Testament?
adults.option_a: It parallels the suicide of Judas Iscariot, who also hanged himself after his counsel led to the betrayal of the Lord's anointed
adults.option_b: It parallels Samson's death in that both involved self-destruction for a greater cause
adults.option_c: It parallels the death of Ananias and Sapphira for dishonesty before God's king
adults.option_d: It parallels the stoning of Stephen in that both were victims of religious political schemes
adults.explanation: Ahithophel, David's trusted friend who betrayed him (paralleling Psalm 41:9, "mine own familiar friend...hath lifted up his heel against me"), hanged himself when his counsel failed (2 Samuel 17:23), mirroring Judas who betrayed Jesus and hanged himself (Matthew 27:5).

adults.question: Where did Ahithophel go to die and what does his making his "house in order" suggest?
adults.option_a: He went to his house in his own city, put his house in order and was buried in his father's sepulchre — suggesting a planned, deliberate death
adults.option_b: He died in the field outside Jerusalem after wandering alone
adults.option_c: He was found dead in his chamber in Jerusalem
adults.option_d: He killed himself on the road to Mahanaim
adults.explanation: Ahithophel put his household in order, and hanged himself, and died, and was buried in the sepulchre of his father (2 Samuel 17:23), indicating a premeditated, organized act of self-destruction.

adults.question: What was the location where David arrived and was received by supporters?
adults.option_a: Mahanaim
adults.option_b: Beersheba
adults.option_c: Jericho
adults.option_d: Gilgal
adults.explanation: David came to Mahanaim (2 Samuel 17:24), the same city that had been Ish-bosheth's capital, where supporters including Shobi, Machir, and Barzillai met him with provisions.

adults.question: Who was leading Absalom's army, and to what earlier commander is he compared in terms of his military role?
adults.option_a: Amasa the son of Ithra, a role paralleling Joab's role as David's commander
adults.option_b: Shimei the Benjamite who led the tribal militia
adults.option_c: Ahithophel himself commanded the vanguard before his death
adults.option_d: Ittai the Gittite who had switched his loyalty to Absalom
adults.explanation: Absalom had made Amasa captain of the host instead of Joab: and Amasa was a man's son, whose name was Ithra an Israelite (2 Samuel 17:25).

adults.question: How does God's sovereignty over human counsel feature in the narrative comment of 2 Samuel 17:14?
adults.option_a: The narrator explicitly states that the LORD appointed to defeat Ahithophel's counsel to bring evil upon Absalom, showing that Absalom's free choice was simultaneously God's judicial sentence
adults.option_b: The passage shows that God rewards the righteous counselor and punishes the wicked one
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that human wisdom cannot prevail when God withholds his blessing
adults.option_d: It illustrates that even the wisest men are subject to the fallibility of human reason
adults.explanation: "For the LORD had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel, to the intent that the LORD might bring evil upon Absalom" (2 Samuel 17:14) — the narrator reveals divine sovereignty working through human deliberation, fulfilling David's prayer in 2 Samuel 15:31.

kids.question: Where did Absalom get caught during the battle?
kids.option_a: His head caught fast in a great oak tree
kids.option_b: He fell into a pit in the forest
kids.option_c: He was surrounded by David's soldiers
kids.option_d: His horse fell and trapped him
kids.explanation: Absalom rode upon a mule, and the mule went under the thick boughs of a great oak, and his head caught hold of the oak, and he was taken up between the heaven and the earth (2 Samuel 18:9).

kids.question: Who killed Absalom while he hung in the tree?
kids.explanation: Joab took three darts in his hand, and thrust them through the heart of Absalom, while he was yet alive in the midst of the oak (2 Samuel 18:14).

kids.question: What did David cry out when he heard that Absalom was dead?
kids.option_a: "O my son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom! would God I had died for thee"
kids.option_b: "The battle is over; let us return to Jerusalem"
kids.option_c: "Thanks be to God who has given us victory"
kids.option_d: "Let Absalom's death be a warning to all rebels"
kids.explanation: The king was much moved, and went up to the chamber over the gate, and wept: and thus he said, "O my son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom! would God I had died for thee, O Absalom, my son, my son!" (2 Samuel 18:33).

teens.question: What specific command did David give his three commanders about Absalom before the battle?
teens.option_a: "Deal gently for my sake with the young man, even with Absalom"
teens.option_b: "Take Absalom alive and bring him to me"
teens.option_c: "Absalom must not escape across the Jordan"
teens.option_d: "Let Absalom be judged by the elders of Israel"
teens.explanation: The king charged Joab and Abishai and Ittai, saying, "Deal gently for my sake with the young man, even with Absalom." And all the people heard when the king gave all the captains charge concerning Absalom (2 Samuel 18:5).

teens.question: Why did the soldier who found Absalom in the tree refuse to kill him?
teens.option_a: He said even for a thousand shekels of silver he would not put forth his hand against the king's son, for the king had commanded to save Absalom
teens.option_b: He feared Absalom would be rescued by his supporters
teens.option_c: He believed it was unlawful to kill a prince
teens.option_d: He wanted to bring Absalom to Joab first
teens.explanation: The man said, "Though I should receive a thousand shekels of silver in mine hand, yet would I not put forth mine hand against the king's son: for in our hearing the king charged thee and Abishai and Ittai, saying, Beware that none touch the young man Absalom" (2 Samuel 18:12).

teens.question: How was the battle described in terms of where more men died?
teens.option_a: The wood devoured more people that day than the sword devoured
teens.option_b: David's army won a great victory in the open field
teens.option_c: Absalom's forces fled to the Jordan and many drowned
teens.option_d: The battle raged for three days in the hills of Ephraim
teens.explanation: The battle was there scattered over the face of all the country: and the wood devoured more people that day than the sword devoured (2 Samuel 18:8).

teens.question: How did Joab respond when the soldier told him he had found Absalom in the tree but refused to kill him?
teens.option_a: Joab said he would not tarry thus with the soldier, took three darts, and thrust them through Absalom's heart himself
teens.option_b: Joab sent ten of his armourbearer to finish the task
teens.option_c: Joab rebuked the soldier for cowardice
teens.option_d: Joab went to David first to ask permission
teens.explanation: Joab said, "I may not tarry thus with thee," and took three darts in his hand, and thrust them through the heart of Absalom (2 Samuel 18:14).

teens.question: Who ran first to bring David news of the battle, and who ran second?
teens.option_a: Ahimaaz the son of Zadok ran first, and Cushi ran second
teens.option_b: Cushi ran first and Ahimaaz ran second
teens.option_c: Joab's armor-bearer ran first and Ahimaaz ran second
teens.option_d: A soldier ran first and Hushai ran second
teens.explanation: Ahimaaz asked Joab to let him run first; Joab sent Cushi first, but then allowed Ahimaaz to run also, and Ahimaaz outran Cushi (2 Samuel 18:19-23).

adults.question: How large were the forces when David numbered the people, and how did he divide them?
adults.option_a: He divided them into three parts: a third under Joab, a third under Abishai the brother of Joab, and a third under Ittai the Gittite
adults.option_b: He put them all under Joab's command
adults.option_c: He divided them into two equal forces guarding both flanks
adults.option_d: He sent a vanguard of twelve thousand and kept the rest in reserve
adults.explanation: David numbered the people...and set captains of thousands and hundreds over them; David sent a third of the people under the hand of Joab, a third under Abishai, and a third under Ittai the Gittite (2 Samuel 18:1-2).

adults.question: What argument did the people use to persuade David not to go into battle himself?
adults.option_a: They said, "Thou shalt not go forth: for if we flee away, they will not care for us; neither if half of us die, will they care for us: but now thou art worth ten thousand of us"
adults.option_b: They said David was too old to fight effectively
adults.option_c: They said David's presence would draw all of Absalom's forces onto one place
adults.option_d: They said the LORD had forbidden David to fight his own son
adults.explanation: The people said, "Thou shalt not go forth...for thou art worth ten thousand of us: therefore now it is better that thou succour us out of the city" (2 Samuel 18:3).

adults.question: What did Joab do with Absalom's body after killing him, and what did the people do?
adults.option_a: Joab cast him into a great pit in the wood and laid a very great heap of stones upon him; all Israel fled every one to his tent
adults.option_b: He left the body hanging in the oak as a warning
adults.option_c: He buried him with honor at the king's command
adults.option_d: He sent the body to David for burial
adults.explanation: Joab cast Absalom into a great pit in the wood, and laid a very great heap of stones upon him: and all Israel fled every one to his tent (2 Samuel 18:17).

adults.question: What had Absalom set up for himself as a monument, and why?
adults.option_a: A pillar in the king's dale, because he had no son to keep his name in remembrance
adults.option_b: A monument in Hebron to mark his coronation site
adults.option_c: A carved stone on the Mount of Olives honoring his mother
adults.option_d: A temple inscription listing his military victories
adults.explanation: Absalom in his lifetime had taken and reared up for himself a pillar in the king's dale: for he said, "I have no son to keep my name in remembrance": and he called the pillar after his own name: and it is called unto this day Absalom's place (2 Samuel 18:18).

adults.question: What message did Ahimaaz bring David, and why was his message incomplete?
adults.option_a: He said there was great tumult but said he knew not what when asked about Absalom; he did not tell David Absalom was dead
adults.option_b: He gave David the full account of Absalom's death immediately
adults.option_c: He told David the battle was won but that Joab had been wounded
adults.option_d: He delivered a sealed letter from Joab without speaking
adults.explanation: Ahimaaz called out that the LORD had blessed the king and delivered the enemies, but when the king asked about Absalom, Ahimaaz said, "When Joab sent the king's servant...I saw a great tumult, but I knew not what it was" (2 Samuel 18:28-29).

adults.question: What was Cushi's report about Absalom to David?
adults.option_a: He said the enemies of my lord the king, and all that rise against thee to do thee hurt, be as that young man is
adults.option_b: He gave a detailed military report of how Joab killed Absalom
adults.option_c: He said Absalom had escaped across the Jordan before the battle ended
adults.option_d: He reported only that the battle was won and said nothing of Absalom
adults.explanation: Cushi said, "The enemies of my lord the king, and all that rise against thee to do thee hurt, be as that young man is" (2 Samuel 18:32).

adults.question: How does 2 Samuel 18 portray the conflict between Joab's military pragmatism and David's paternal grief?
adults.option_a: Joab kills Absalom in defiance of the king's explicit command, judging the military necessity above the king's personal wishes
adults.option_b: Both Joab and David agree that Absalom must die but disagree on the manner of execution
adults.option_c: Joab is shown to be righteous in killing Absalom because Absalom was a rebel
adults.option_d: David's command to spare Absalom is overruled by the elders of Israel who demand Absalom's death
adults.explanation: David commanded all three captains, "Deal gently...with Absalom" (2 Samuel 18:5), yet Joab killed him with his own hand (18:14), reasoning that Absalom alive would be a perpetual threat — a choice that earned Joab David's bitter grief and later David's command to Solomon to deal with Joab (1 Kings 2:5-6).

adults.question: What literary and theological significance does David's lament "O my son Absalom!" carry in the broader narrative?
adults.option_a: It shows David's personal anguish eclipsing his joy as king, and echoes his role as a father who loved a rebel son — a type of God's grief over wayward children
adults.option_b: It demonstrates that David had secretly planned to pardon Absalom after the war
adults.option_c: It shows David's unfitness to continue as king because he could not subordinate emotion to duty
adults.option_d: It is primarily a political statement mourning the loss of his heir apparent
adults.explanation: David's sevenfold cry "O my son Absalom!" (2 Samuel 18:33, 19:4) reveals a father's love that outlasts betrayal. Theologians see in it a picture of God's longsuffering grief over human rebellion, and the text became a model for penitential literature.

kids.question: Who rebuked David for mourning too much and told him he had shamed his soldiers?
kids.explanation: Joab came into the house to the king, and said...thou hast shamed this day the faces of all thy servants...arise, go forth, and speak comfortably unto thy servants (2 Samuel 19:5-7).

kids.question: Who was the man who had cursed David with stones, and what did David say about punishing him when he came to beg forgiveness?
kids.option_a: Shimei the son of Gera, and David swore that he would not kill him
kids.option_b: Mephibosheth, and David gave him back his lands
kids.option_c: Ziba, and David forgave him his debt
kids.option_d: Amasa, and David made him a commander
kids.explanation: Shimei the son of Gera fell down before the king, and the king said, "Thou shalt not die": and the king sware unto him (2 Samuel 19:18, 23).

kids.question: Who was the very old man from Gilead who had helped David and was now offered a reward but chose to go home instead?
kids.explanation: Barzillai the Gileadite came down from Rogelim...and the king said unto Barzillai, "Come thou over with me," but Barzillai asked to be allowed to go back to his own city (2 Samuel 19:31-37).

teens.question: What threat did Joab make to David if he did not go out and encourage his troops after Absalom's death?
teens.option_a: He said if David did not arise, not a man would remain with him that night, and this would be worse for David than all the evil that had befallen him
teens.option_b: He said he would take command of the kingdom himself
teens.option_c: He threatened to leave David's service with all his soldiers
teens.option_d: He said the men of Israel would elect a new king by morning
teens.explanation: Joab told David, "If thou go not forth, there will not tarry one with thee this night: and that will be worse unto thee than all the evil that befell thee from thy youth until now" (2 Samuel 19:7).

teens.question: What happened concerning Amasa that David arranged before crossing back to Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: David said to Amasa, "Art thou not of my bone, and of my flesh?" and made him captain of the host instead of Joab
teens.option_b: David had Amasa arrested for leading Absalom's army
teens.option_c: David gave Amasa a pardon but stripped him of all rank
teens.option_d: David sent Amasa back to lead Judah's army from Hebron
teens.explanation: David said to Amasa, "Art thou not of my bone, and of my flesh? God do so to me, and more also, if thou be not captain of the host before me continually in the room of Joab" (2 Samuel 19:13).

teens.question: What argument broke out between Israel and Judah as they brought David back?
teens.option_a: Israel accused Judah of stealing the king, and Judah said the king was near of kin to them; Israel replied they had ten parts in the king and were first to bring him back
teens.option_b: The two sides argued about who should get the larger share of David's gifts
teens.option_c: Israel accused Judah of refusing to fight against Absalom
teens.option_d: Judah accused Israel of secretly supporting Absalom
teens.explanation: The men of Israel said to Judah, "We have ten parts in the king...why then did ye despise us?" And the men of Judah answered the men of Israel (2 Samuel 19:43).

teens.question: What did Mephibosheth tell David about why he had not accompanied him in flight?
teens.option_a: He said his servant Ziba had deceived him; Mephibosheth had saddled an ass to go with David but Ziba had slandered him
teens.option_b: He said he was too ill to travel on foot
teens.option_c: He said the priests had told him to remain in Jerusalem as a witness
teens.option_d: He said he feared being captured by Absalom on the road
teens.explanation: Mephibosheth said, "My lord, O king, my servant did deceive me: for thy servant said, I will saddle me an ass, that I may ride thereon, and go to the king; because thy servant is lame. And he hath slandered thy servant unto my lord the king" (2 Samuel 19:26-27).

teens.question: How old was Barzillai and what reasons did he give for not going to Jerusalem with David?
teens.option_a: He was fourscore years old and said he could not discern good and evil, nor taste what he ate or drank, nor hear the voice of singing men and women
teens.option_b: He was seventy years old and said his family needed him in Gilead
teens.option_c: He was sixty years old and said his health was too poor to make the journey
teens.option_d: He was ninety years old and said God had forbidden him to leave his ancestral land
teens.explanation: Barzillai said to the king, "I am this day fourscore years old: and can I discern between good and evil? can thy servant taste what I eat or what I drink? can I hear any more the voice of singing men and singing women?" (2 Samuel 19:35).

adults.question: What position did David take while mourning, according to the report that reached Joab's soldiers?
adults.option_a: The king covered his face and cried with a loud voice, "O my son Absalom, O Absalom, my son, my son!"
adults.option_b: David shut himself in the chamber over the gate and refused all food for three days
adults.option_c: David sat in the throne room weeping and tearing his robe
adults.option_d: David went out alone to the Jordan riverbank
adults.explanation: The king covered his face, and the king cried with a loud voice, "O my son Absalom, O Absalom, my son, my son!" (2 Samuel 19:4).

adults.question: What political problem did David face in re-establishing his rule over Judah, and how did he solve it?
adults.option_a: Judah had not invited David back; he sent to Zadok and Abiathar to ask the elders of Judah why they were last to bring him home, and sweetened it by promising to make Amasa his captain
adults.option_b: Israel was blocking David's crossing of the Jordan
adults.option_c: The priests refused to restore David because of his sin with Bathsheba
adults.option_d: Joab threatened to support a rival claimant unless David returned quickly
adults.explanation: David sent to Zadok and Abiathar, "Speak unto the elders of Judah, saying, Why are ye the last to bring the king back to his house? ye are my brethren, ye are my bones and my flesh: wherefore then are ye the last?" And he promised Amasa the captaincy (2 Samuel 19:11-13).

adults.question: What was the theological issue Abishai raised when he objected to pardoning Shimei?
adults.option_a: He said Shimei should die because he cursed the LORD's anointed
adults.option_b: He said pardoning Shimei would signal weakness to Israel's enemies
adults.option_c: He appealed to the Mosaic law against cursing the king
adults.option_d: He said it would dishonor the memory of Absalom to pardon his supporter
adults.explanation: Abishai said, "Shall not Shimei be put to death for this, because he cursed the LORD's anointed?" (2 Samuel 19:21).

adults.question: What reason did David give for pardoning Shimei on the day of his return?
adults.option_a: He said shall any man be put to death in Israel this day, knowing that today I am king over Israel
adults.option_b: He said he was bound by his oath and could not go back on his word
adults.option_c: He said God had commanded him in a dream to show mercy
adults.option_d: He said Shimei was old and near death and would soon answer to God
adults.explanation: David said, "Shall there any man be put to death this day in Israel? for do not I know that I am this day king over Israel?" (2 Samuel 19:22).

adults.question: What did David say to Mephibosheth about the land dispute with Ziba, and what does Mephibosheth's answer reveal?
adults.option_a: David said they should divide the land; Mephibosheth said Ziba could take it all since the king had returned in peace — revealing Mephibosheth's true loyalty
adults.option_b: David restored all the land to Mephibosheth and punished Ziba
adults.option_c: David told Mephibosheth the matter was closed and he would not revisit it
adults.option_d: David gave all the land to Ziba as he had already sworn
adults.explanation: David said, "Why speakest thou any more of thy matters? I have said, Thou and Ziba divide the land." Mephibosheth said, "Yea, let him take all, forasmuch as my lord the king is come again in peace" (2 Samuel 19:29-30).

adults.question: What symbolic gesture of loyalty did Chimham (Barzillai's son) represent in the narrative?
adults.option_a: Barzillai sent Chimham in his own place to go with David to Jerusalem, so David could bless him there, showing generational transfer of loyalty from old ally to young heir
adults.option_b: Chimham carried David's royal regalia back to Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Chimham was Barzillai's slave whom he freed as a gift to David
