adults.option_d: Chimham was appointed governor of the region east of Jordan as a reward
adults.explanation: Barzillai said, "Behold thy servant Chimham; let him go over with my lord the king; and do to him what shall seem good unto thee" (2 Samuel 19:37-38).

adults.question: What does Joab's rebuke of David in chapter 19 reveal about the tension between kingly grief and royal duty?
adults.option_a: Joab argues that David's public mourning was shaming the soldiers who had saved his life and threatening the stability of the kingdom
adults.option_b: Joab argues that David was grieving too long for a traitor
adults.option_c: Joab challenges David's right to mourn a son who had tried to kill him
adults.option_d: Joab's rebuke was primarily a political power move to restore his own influence
adults.explanation: Joab told David that he had shamed those who had saved his life and the lives of his household, and that David seemed to love those who hated him and hate those who loved him, warning that no one would be left if David did not act (2 Samuel 19:5-7).

adults.question: How does the quarrel between Israel and Judah at the end of chapter 19 foreshadow the permanent division of the kingdom?
adults.option_a: The tribal jealousy over who had more right to the king — Israel's "ten parts" versus Judah's blood kinship — reveals the fault line that Sheba exploits in chapter 20 and that splits the kingdom under Rehoboam in 1 Kings 12
adults.option_b: It shows that the northern tribes had accepted Absalom's rebellion and were being punished
adults.option_c: It is merely a protocol dispute over ceremony with no deeper significance
adults.option_d: It shows that Joab's army from Judah had been favored while Israel's men were excluded
adults.explanation: The men of Israel complained that Judah had stolen the king (2 Samuel 19:41-43), and the words of Israel were fiercer than the words of Judah. This rivalry directly triggers Sheba's revolt (20:1-2) and previews the Israel-Judah split (1 Kings 12:16).

kids.question: What did Sheba the son of Bichri say when he blew a trumpet and started a new rebellion?
kids.option_a: "We have no part in David, neither have we inheritance in the son of Jesse"
kids.option_c: "Let us return to our tents, O Israel"
kids.explanation: Sheba blew a trumpet, and said, "We have no part in David, neither have we inheritance in the son of Jesse: every man to his tents, O Israel" (2 Samuel 20:1).

kids.question: How did the wise woman of Abel save her city?
kids.option_a: She persuaded the people to cut off Sheba's head and throw it over the wall to Joab
kids.option_b: She opened the gates and negotiated a peace treaty
kids.option_c: She hid Sheba in the city until Joab left
kids.option_d: She sent a message to David asking for mercy
kids.explanation: The woman went unto all the people in her wisdom: and they cut off the head of Sheba the son of Bichri, and cast it out to Joab (2 Samuel 20:22).

kids.question: Who did Joab kill treacherously at the start of chapter 20?
kids.explanation: Joab took Amasa by the beard with the right hand to kiss him, and smote him with a sword in the fifth rib, and he died (2 Samuel 20:9-10).

teens.question: What task had David given Amasa that he failed to complete on time?
teens.option_a: David told Amasa to assemble the men of Judah within three days to pursue Sheba, but Amasa tarried beyond the set time
teens.option_b: David sent Amasa to negotiate a peace with Sheba
teens.option_c: David ordered Amasa to fortify the northern border cities
teens.option_d: David told Amasa to take a census of all able-bodied men
teens.explanation: David said to Amasa, "Assemble me the men of Judah within three days, and be thou here present." So Amasa went to assemble the men of Judah: but he tarried longer than the set time (2 Samuel 20:4-5).

teens.question: Why was the pursuit of Sheba considered urgent by David?
teens.option_a: He said Sheba would do more harm than Absalom, and must not get himself into fenced cities and escape
teens.option_b: He said Sheba had allied with the Philistines
teens.option_c: He said God had told him Sheba would overthrow the kingdom
teens.option_d: He said Sheba had stolen the ark of the covenant
teens.explanation: David said to Abishai, "Now shall Sheba...do us more harm than did Absalom: take thou thy lord's servants, and pursue after him, lest he get him fenced cities, and escape us" (2 Samuel 20:6).

teens.question: What did the wise woman of Abel call her city as she persuaded Joab not to destroy it?
teens.option_a: She called it a mother in Israel and a city peaceable and faithful, and asked why he would swallow up the inheritance of the LORD
teens.option_b: She called it a city of priests dedicated to God
teens.option_c: She called it the oldest city in the tribe of Dan
teens.option_d: She called it a city of refuge protected by the law of Moses
teens.explanation: The woman said, "I am one of them that are peaceable and faithful in Israel: thou seekest to destroy a city and a mother in Israel: why wilt thou swallow up the inheritance of the LORD?" (2 Samuel 20:19).

teens.question: What had Joab's men done to Amasa's body in the road after killing him?
teens.option_a: He wallowed in blood in the midst of the highway, and a man covered him with a cloth when he saw the people stopped
teens.option_b: They buried him honorably at the side of the road
teens.option_c: They left him there publicly as a warning to other rebels
teens.option_d: They carried his body back to Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Amasa wallowed in blood in the midst of the highway...when the man saw that all the people stood still, he removed Amasa out of the highway into the field, and cast a cloth upon him (2 Samuel 20:12).

teens.question: What was the list of David's chief officers given at the end of chapter 20?
teens.option_a: Joab over the host, Benaiah over the Cherethites and Pelethites, Adoram over the tribute, Jehoshaphat the recorder, Sheva the scribe, Zadok and Abiathar the priests, and Ira the chief ruler
teens.option_b: Amasa over the host, Hushai over counsel, Nathan the prophet, and Abiathar the priest
teens.option_c: Abishai over the host, Joab over the treasury, and Zadok the high priest
teens.option_d: Ittai over the host, Joab over the body guard, and Nathan the recorder
teens.explanation: So Joab was over all the host of Israel: and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada was over the Cherethites and over the Pelethites: And Adoram was over the tribute: and Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud was recorder: And Sheva was scribe: and Zadok and Abiathar were the priests: And also Ira the Jairite was a chief ruler about David (2 Samuel 20:23-26).

adults.question: What was the ethnic identity of Sheba and why was it significant to the tribal politics of the day?
adults.option_a: Sheba was a Benjamite, the tribe of Saul, and his revolt tapped into the same tribal resentment that had supported Ish-bosheth's rival kingdom
adults.option_b: Sheba was from the tribe of Ephraim and led the northern rebellion
adults.option_c: Sheba was from the tribe of Gad and had supported Absalom before this
adults.option_d: Sheba was a Canaanite mercenary who exploited the civil war for personal gain
adults.explanation: Sheba the son of Bichri, a Benjamite (2 Samuel 20:1) — the tribe of Saul — exploited the quarrel between Israel and Judah (19:41-43), and all the men of Israel followed him (20:2), showing that Saul's tribal loyalties had never fully transferred to David.

adults.question: What was the situation of the ten concubines David had left to keep the house, after his return?
adults.option_a: David fed them but went not in unto them, and they were shut up unto the day of their death, living in widowhood
adults.option_b: David restored them to full status as concubines in his household
adults.option_c: David sent them to their families with a provision of silver
adults.option_d: David gave them to his sons as wives to cement political alliances
adults.explanation: David came to his house at Jerusalem; and the king took the ten women his concubines...and put them in ward, and fed them, but went not in unto them. So they were shut up unto the day of their death, living in widowhood (2 Samuel 20:3).

adults.question: How did Joab use the pretense of greeting Amasa with a kiss to kill him?
adults.option_a: He took Amasa by the beard with his right hand as if to kiss him, while his sword fell out in his left hand and he smote Amasa in the fifth rib without a second blow
adults.option_b: He distracted Amasa with conversation and then stabbed him from behind
adults.option_c: He arranged an ambush at a city gate disguised as a military parade
adults.option_d: He poisoned the wine cup he shared with Amasa before the meeting
adults.explanation: Joab said, "Art thou in health, my brother?" and took Amasa by the beard with the right hand to kiss him. But Amasa took no heed to the sword that was in Joab's hand: and he smote him therewith in the fifth rib (2 Samuel 20:9-10).

adults.question: What does the negotiation between Joab and the wise woman of Abel illustrate about ancient Israelite city law?
adults.option_a: It shows that an accused city could first seek terms of peace before being attacked, reflecting the principle of Deuteronomy 20:10-12, and that cities had wise leaders empowered to surrender wanted men
adults.option_b: It shows that women held formal judicial authority in Israelite cities
adults.option_c: It illustrates the custom of ransom negotiations before a siege began
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that Joab was bound by law to accept surrender of individuals before destroying a city
adults.explanation: The wise woman negotiated on behalf of the city (2 Samuel 20:16-22), reflecting the law that requires an offer of peace before attacking an Israelite city (Deuteronomy 20:10-12); she identified Sheba as the guilty party and secured the city's safety by delivering his head.

adults.question: What ancient proverb did the wise woman invoke in her negotiation with Joab?
adults.option_a: She said, "They were wont to speak in old time, saying, They shall surely ask counsel at Abel: and so they ended the matter"
adults.option_b: She said, "A city that opens its gates to peace shall not be destroyed"
adults.option_c: She quoted, "The blood of the innocent cries out from the ground"
adults.option_d: She said, "Where the king's word is, there is power"
adults.explanation: The woman said, "They were wont to speak in old time, saying, They shall surely ask counsel at Abel: and so they ended the matter" (2 Samuel 20:18).

adults.question: What does Joab's killing of Amasa in chapter 20 reveal about Joab's character and his grip on power?
adults.option_a: It reveals that Joab was unwilling to be displaced from his position as commander, eliminating any rival David appointed — having already killed Abner and Absalom
adults.option_b: It shows Joab was acting on secret orders from David who resented Amasa
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that Amasa was genuinely a traitor who deserved to die
adults.option_d: It reveals that Joab feared Amasa would expose his killing of Absalom against David's orders
adults.explanation: Joab had already killed Abner (2 Samuel 3:27) when David made Abner his commander, and now kills Amasa (20:10) when David appointed Amasa over Joab; in both cases Joab used treacherous deceit to eliminate rivals, and David acknowledged this pattern on his deathbed (1 Kings 2:5).

adults.question: What is the significance of the detail that the soldier who stood by Amasa's body said, "He that favoureth Joab, and he that is for David, let him go after Joab"?
adults.option_a: It shows the soldiers equated loyalty to Joab with loyalty to David, reflecting Joab's power over the army despite his crimes
adults.option_b: It reveals that the soldiers were confused about who was in command after Amasa's death
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that the men of Judah had transferred their loyalty from David to Joab
adults.option_d: It shows that Joab had publicly announced his claim to lead the army
adults.explanation: A man of Joab's men stood by Amasa and said, "He that favoureth Joab, and he that is for David, let him go after Joab" (2 Samuel 20:11), showing that Joab had made himself synonymous with David's cause to maintain control.

adults.question: How does 2 Samuel 20 bring the Absalom rebellion narrative to a full structural close?
adults.option_a: The chapter resolves the political crisis (Sheba suppressed), restores the administrative list (David's officers reestablished), and returns Joab to power — completing the frame opened by Absalom's revolt in chapter 15
adults.option_b: The chapter ends with David repenting publicly for all sins committed during the rebellion
adults.option_c: The chapter closes with a covenant renewal ceremony at the tabernacle
adults.option_d: The chapter ends with the oracle of Nathan confirming David's restored standing before God
adults.explanation: Chapter 20 ends the long rebellion narrative (chapters 13-20) with Sheba killed (20:22), David's officers listed (20:23-26), and Joab back in command — forming a literary and political closure that mirrors the stable reign described before the rebellion began in chapter 11.

kids.question: Why was there a famine for three years in Israel?
kids.option_a: Because Saul and his house had killed the Gibeonites, breaking a covenant Israel had made with them
kids.option_b: Because the people had worshipped idols
kids.option_c: Because there was no rain for three years
kids.option_d: Because David had sinned in the matter of Bathsheba
kids.explanation: David sought the face of the LORD, and the LORD answered, "It is for Saul, and for his bloody house, because he slew the Gibeonites" (2 Samuel 21:1).

kids.question: Who watched over the bodies of Saul's sons on the hill, keeping the birds and beasts away?
kids.option_a: Rizpah, the daughter of Aiah, Saul's concubine
kids.explanation: Rizpah the daughter of Aiah took sackcloth, and spread it for her upon the rock, from the beginning of harvest until water dropped upon them out of heaven, and suffered neither the birds of the air to rest on them by day, nor the beasts of the field by night (2 Samuel 21:10).

kids.question: What happened to David when he was weary fighting a Philistine giant?
kids.option_a: Abishai came and helped David and smote the Philistine and killed him
kids.option_b: David's sword broke but he escaped
kids.option_d: God blinded the giant and David escaped
kids.explanation: Ishbi-benob thought to kill David; but Abishai the son of Zeruiah succoured David, and smote the Philistine, and killed him (2 Samuel 21:16-17).

teens.question: What did the Gibeonites ask of David to atone for Saul's slaughter of them?
teens.option_a: Let seven men of Saul's sons be hanged up unto the LORD in Gibeah of Saul
teens.option_b: A payment of silver and gold from the royal treasury
teens.option_c: The rebuilding of the Gibeonite cities Saul had destroyed
teens.option_d: The right to rule over the land of Benjamin
teens.explanation: The Gibeonites said, "Let seven men of his sons be delivered unto us, and we will hang them up unto the LORD in Gibeah of Saul" (2 Samuel 21:6).

teens.question: Which of Saul's descendants did David spare and why?
teens.option_a: He spared Mephibosheth the son of Jonathan because of the LORD's oath between David and Jonathan
teens.option_b: He spared Michal because she was David's wife
teens.option_c: He spared Ish-bosheth's son because he was the last heir
teens.option_d: He spared Armoni because he had helped David during the flight from Saul
teens.explanation: David spared Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan the son of Saul, because of the LORD's oath that was between them (2 Samuel 21:7).

teens.question: What did David do when he was told about Rizpah's vigil over the bodies?
teens.option_a: He gathered the bones of Saul and Jonathan from Jabesh-gilead and buried them with the bones of the hanged men in the sepulchre of Kish
teens.option_b: He immediately ordered all the bodies buried in a royal tomb
teens.option_c: He sent a military escort to guard Rizpah and the bodies
teens.option_d: He went himself to Gibeah to mourn over the dead
teens.explanation: David went and took the bones of Saul and the bones of Jonathan from the men of Jabesh-gilead, and gathered the bones of them that were hanged; they buried the bones of Saul and Jonathan in the country of Benjamin in the sepulchre of Kish (2 Samuel 21:12-14).

teens.question: What was the outcome of David's men telling him he must not go out to battle anymore after he nearly died?
teens.option_a: They said he must not quench the light of Israel, and thereafter the battles with the Philistines were fought by David's mighty men
teens.option_b: David agreed and appointed Joab as permanent commander in his place
teens.option_c: David agreed to only direct battles from a tent in the rear
teens.option_d: David refused and continued fighting personally despite their warning
teens.explanation: David's men sware unto him, saying, "Thou shalt go no more out with us to battle, that thou quench not the light of Israel" (2 Samuel 21:17). The subsequent giants were all slain by David's servants (21:18-22).

teens.question: How many Philistine giants are recorded as falling in the battles described at the end of chapter 21?
teens.option_a: Four, all of them fell at the hand of David and his servants
teens.option_b: Seven
teens.option_c: Three
teens.option_d: Ten
teens.explanation: These four were born to the giant in Gath, and fell by the hand of David, and by the hand of his servants (2 Samuel 21:22).

adults.question: What was the ancient covenant that Saul had broken regarding the Gibeonites, and how had Israel originally entered it?
adults.option_a: Joshua had sworn to spare the Gibeonites who had deceived Israel; Saul violated this by seeking to slay them in his zeal for Israel and Judah
adults.option_b: Moses had made a covenant with the Gibeonites at Sinai
adults.option_c: David himself had personally promised Saul not to harm the Gibeonites
adults.option_d: The covenant had been made by the elders of Israel at the time of the judges
adults.explanation: The Gibeonites were not of the children of Israel, but of the remnant of the Amorites: and the children of Israel had sworn unto them; and Saul sought to slay them in his zeal (2 Samuel 21:2), referring to Joshua's covenant in Joshua 9:15-19.

adults.question: During what period were the seven bodies exposed, and what natural sign marked the end of the judgment?
adults.option_a: They were exposed at the beginning of barley harvest until water dropped upon them out of heaven, signaling the end of drought
adults.option_b: They were exposed for forty days and nights during a great storm
adults.option_c: They were exposed from Passover to the Feast of Tabernacles
adults.option_d: They were exposed for a full year until a sign appeared in the heavens
adults.explanation: Rizpah watched from the beginning of harvest until water dropped upon them out of heaven (2 Samuel 21:10), and God was intreated for the land after the bones of Saul, Jonathan, and the seven were buried (21:14).

adults.question: What was the weight of the spear of Ishbi-benob the Philistine giant who nearly killed David?
adults.option_a: Three hundred shekels of brass
adults.option_b: Five hundred shekels of iron
adults.option_c: Two hundred shekels of iron
adults.option_d: A talent of brass
adults.explanation: Ishbi-benob...the weight of whose spear weighed three hundred shekels of brass in weight, he being girded with a new sword, thought to have slain David (2 Samuel 21:16).

adults.question: Who killed Lahmi the brother of Goliath, and what was distinctive about Lahmi's weapon?
adults.option_a: Elhanan the son of Jaare-oregim, a Bethlehemite, slew him; his spear staff was like a weaver's beam
adults.option_b: Abishai killed him with a sling and stone like David had killed Goliath
adults.option_c: Joab killed him in a duel before the battle lines
adults.option_d: Jonathan son of Shimeah killed him while on patrol near Gath
adults.explanation: Elhanan the son of Jaare-oregim, a Bethlehemite, slew the brother of Goliath the Gittite, the staff of whose spear was like a weaver's beam (2 Samuel 21:19).

adults.question: What was notable about the giant at Gath who had six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot?
adults.option_a: He had on every hand six fingers, and on every foot six toes, four and twenty in number, and he defied Israel; Jonathan the son of Shimeah slew him
adults.option_b: He was the tallest man ever recorded in Israelite history
adults.option_c: He wielded two swords simultaneously because of his extra digits
adults.option_d: He had defeated three previous champions before Jonathan fought him
adults.explanation: And yet again there was a battle in Gath, where was a man of great stature, that had on every hand six fingers, and on every foot six toes, four and twenty in number...and Jonathan the son of Shimeah the brother of David slew him (2 Samuel 21:20-21).

adults.question: What theological principle does the Gibeonite episode illustrate regarding covenant obligations?
adults.option_a: That covenant oaths bind a nation across generations and that breaking them brings divine judgment, even when the original covenant was made by deception
adults.option_b: That individual sins affect only the sinner and not future generations
adults.option_c: That God judges only the generation that commits the sin
adults.option_d: That political necessity can override covenant obligations
adults.explanation: Though the Gibeonite covenant had been made by deception (Joshua 9), Israel was still bound by the oath sworn in the LORD's name (Joshua 9:19); Saul's violation brought famine on his entire nation (2 Samuel 21:1-2), demonstrating that covenant oaths have binding divine weight.

adults.question: What does Rizpah's vigil over the exposed bodies communicate about maternal loyalty and its effect on David?
adults.option_a: Her months-long vigil protecting the bodies moved David to give Saul and his house a proper burial, showing that courageous love can prompt justice from the powerful
adults.option_b: Her vigil was a formal religious rite required by Mosaic law for atonement
adults.option_c: Her action was considered shameful by the people of Israel
adults.option_d: Her vigil had no effect on David and only ended with the rains
adults.explanation: Rizpah kept her vigil from barley harvest until rain fell (2 Samuel 21:10), and when David was told what she had done, he was moved to gather and bury the bones of Saul, Jonathan, and the seven, and "after that God was intreated for the land" (21:14).

adults.question: How does the chapter's structure — opening with a covenant violated by Saul and closing with giants defeated by David's men — serve the theological arc of 1-2 Samuel?
adults.option_a: It shows that the consequences of Saul's unfaithfulness (famine, covenant breach) are being resolved under David, while David's own vulnerability (he nearly died) is covered by loyal servants, pointing to God's grace sustaining the Davidic line
adults.option_b: It is primarily an appendix with no theological connection to the main narrative
adults.option_c: It shows that David had fully atoned for all previous sins in this chapter
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that military victories alone guarantee divine blessing for a dynasty
adults.explanation: Chapter 21 pairs the resolution of Saul's blood guilt (atonement by execution and burial, 21:1-14) with David's preserved life (Abishai saves him, 21:17) and his servants' victories over giants (21:18-22), showing covenant faithfulness (David to Jonathan) and God's protection of the Davidic promise despite human frailty.

kids.question: What did David call the LORD at the very beginning of his song of praise?
kids.option_a: My rock, and my fortress, and my deliverer
kids.explanation: David spoke unto the LORD the words of this song...and he said, "The LORD is my rock, and my fortress, and my deliverer" (2 Samuel 22:1-2).

kids.question: What did David say about the LORD in verse 7 when he was in distress?
kids.option_a: He said he cried to the LORD and the LORD heard his voice from his temple
kids.option_b: He said he prayed in silence and the LORD answered in a dream
kids.option_c: He said he called to his servants to pray for him
kids.option_d: He said the LORD sent an angel to comfort him
kids.explanation: David said, "In my distress I called upon the LORD...he did hear my voice out of his temple, and my cry did enter into his ears" (2 Samuel 22:7).

kids.question: What does David say the LORD rewards people for at the end of the psalm?
kids.option_a: He rewards the merciful with mercy and the upright according to their uprightness
kids.option_b: He rewards the richest people the most
kids.option_c: He rewards only kings and priests
kids.option_d: He rewards those who offer the most sacrifices
kids.explanation: David said, "With the merciful thou wilt shew thyself merciful, and with the upright man thou wilt shew thyself upright" (2 Samuel 22:26).

teens.question: What dramatic imagery does David use to describe God coming to his rescue in the song?
teens.option_a: The earth shook, smoke went out of God's nostrils, fire devoured, he rode on a cherub and flew upon the wings of the wind
teens.option_b: God appeared as a pillar of fire and led David through the wilderness
teens.option_c: God sent a great flood that drowned David's enemies
teens.option_d: God appeared as a still small voice and spoke to David's heart
teens.explanation: David sang, "The earth shook and trembled...There went up a smoke out of his nostrils...He rode upon a cherub, and did fly: and he was seen upon the wings of the wind" (2 Samuel 22:8-11).

teens.question: What does David say about his own uprightness as the reason for God's deliverance?
teens.option_a: He says the LORD recompensed him according to his righteousness, because he kept the ways of the LORD and did not wickedly depart from his God
teens.option_b: He says God saved him despite his many sins
teens.option_c: He says he was delivered because he was king of Israel
teens.option_d: He says God saved him because the people prayed for him
teens.explanation: David sang, "The LORD rewarded me according to my righteousness; according to the cleanness of my hands hath he recompensed me. For I have kept the ways of the LORD, and have not wickedly departed from my God" (2 Samuel 22:21-22).

teens.question: What does David say about the lamp of salvation in this song?
teens.option_a: He says the LORD will lighten his darkness and that by God he can run through a troop and leap over a wall
teens.option_b: He says the LORD's lamp will never go out in the temple
teens.option_c: He says God's word is a lamp to his feet on the battlefield
teens.option_d: He says the LORD kindled a great fire of victory before his army
teens.explanation: David sang, "For thou art my lamp, O LORD: and the LORD will lighten my darkness. For by thee I have run through a troop: by my God have I leaped over a wall" (2 Samuel 22:29-30).

teens.question: What military imagery does David use to describe God's training of him for battle?
teens.option_a: He says God teaches his hands to war and his fingers to fight, and that God's right hand upholds him
teens.option_b: He says God gave him a sword that never grew dull
teens.option_c: He says God drove back the enemy host with arrows from heaven
teens.option_d: He says God gave him wisdom to devise clever battle strategies
teens.explanation: David sang, "He teacheth my hands to war; so that a bow of steel is broken by mine arms...thou hast also given me the shield of thy salvation: and thy gentleness hath made me great" (2 Samuel 22:35-36).

teens.question: What does David declare about the LORD's way at the end of his great song?
teens.option_a: "As for God, his way is perfect: the word of the LORD is tried: he is a buckler to all them that trust in him"
teens.option_b: "The LORD is righteous in all his judgments and gracious in all his ways"
teens.option_c: "God alone is the fortress and none can stand against his might"
teens.option_d: "Happy is the nation whose God is the LORD, and the people he has chosen"
teens.explanation: David sang, "As for God, his way is perfect; the word of the LORD is tried: he is a buckler to all them that trust in him" (2 Samuel 22:31).

adults.question: In what event context does the narrator say David sang this song?
adults.option_a: In the day that the LORD had delivered him out of the hand of all his enemies, and out of the hand of Saul
adults.option_b: After his sin with Bathsheba had been forgiven
adults.option_c: After the death of Absalom and the suppression of all rebellion
adults.option_d: On the night before the great battle with the Philistines
adults.explanation: David spake unto the LORD the words of this song in the day that the LORD had delivered him out of the hand of all his enemies, and out of the hand of Saul (2 Samuel 22:1).

adults.question: What does David call God using six distinct metaphorical titles in verses 2-3?
adults.option_a: Rock, fortress, deliverer, God, strength/shield, horn of salvation, and high tower
adults.option_b: King, shepherd, judge, redeemer, saviour, and father
adults.option_c: Light, fire, wind, rock, shield, and tower
adults.option_d: LORD, God, Almighty, Most High, Everlasting, and Holy One
adults.explanation: David said, "The LORD is my rock, and my fortress, and my deliverer; The God of my rock; in him will I trust: he is my shield, and the horn of my salvation, my high tower, and my refuge, my saviour" (2 Samuel 22:2-3).

adults.question: How does David describe the nature of God's knowledge of and response to his distress in verse 7?
adults.option_a: He says he called to the LORD in his distress and the LORD heard his voice from his temple and his cry entered God's ears
adults.option_b: He says God foreknew his distress and prepared a way of escape before he prayed
adults.option_c: He says the LORD appeared to him in a vision before the battle to promise victory
adults.option_d: He says God spoke through Nathan the prophet to comfort him in his hour of need
adults.explanation: David sang, "In my distress I called upon the LORD, and cried to my God: and he did hear my voice out of his temple, and my cry did enter into his ears" (2 Samuel 22:7).

adults.question: What does David say God brought him into regarding his final state of deliverance?
adults.option_a: "He brought me forth also into a large place: he delivered me, because he delighted in me"
adults.option_b: "He brought me into the sanctuary and gave me rest from my enemies"
adults.option_c: "He placed me upon the throne and anointed me a second time before all Israel"
adults.option_d: "He brought me to the mountain of God and showed me his glory"
adults.explanation: David sang, "He brought me forth also into a large place; he delivered me, because he delighted in me" (2 Samuel 22:20).

adults.question: What does David say about the froward (perverse) in God's dealing with them?
adults.option_a: "With the froward thou wilt shew thyself unsavoury"
adults.option_b: "The froward shall be cut off from the land"
adults.option_c: "God turns away his face from the froward"
adults.option_d: "The froward shall not stand in the judgment"
adults.explanation: David sang, "And with the froward thou wilt shew thyself unsavoury" (2 Samuel 22:27), meaning God deals with the perverse according to their own crooked nature.

adults.question: What does David's declaration about his enemies in verses 38-43 emphasize about the completeness of his victory?
adults.option_a: He consumed them, wounded them so they could not arise, beat them as small as the dust of the earth, stamped them as the mire of the street, and spread them abroad
adults.option_b: He showed mercy to his enemies and offered them peace after victory
adults.option_c: He took them as prisoners and put them to useful labor for the kingdom
adults.option_d: He drove them out of the land and set border markers against their return
adults.explanation: David sang, "I have pursued mine enemies, and destroyed them...I have wounded them that they could not arise...I did beat them as small as the dust of the earth, I did stamp them as the mire of the street, and did spread them abroad" (2 Samuel 22:38-43).

adults.question: How does chapter 22 relate to Psalm 18 in the Psalter?
adults.option_a: The two texts are nearly identical, showing that this song was preserved in both the historical narrative and the Psalms collection, affirming its authenticity as a Davidic composition
adults.option_b: Chapter 22 is a later summary of Psalm 18 composed by a court scribe
adults.option_c: Psalm 18 is a shorter liturgical version while 2 Samuel 22 is the original battle hymn
adults.option_d: The texts share only the opening verse and diverge completely afterward
adults.explanation: 2 Samuel 22 and Psalm 18 are parallel texts with only minor variant readings, demonstrating that this was a genuine Davidic psalm that was incorporated into both the Samuel history and the Psalms collection.

adults.question: What messianic significance do commentators find in the closing verse of chapter 22 about David's seed?
adults.option_a: God shows mercy to David and to his seed for evermore, pointing forward to the eternal Davidic covenant and ultimately to Christ as David's eternal heir
adults.option_b: The closing verse applies only to David's immediate physical descendants ruling in Judah
adults.option_c: The promise of mercy to David's seed was conditional on the seed keeping the law perfectly
adults.option_d: The closing verse is a later addition added after the exile to comfort the returning captives
adults.explanation: David sang, "He is the tower of salvation for his king: and sheweth mercy to his anointed, unto David, and to his seed for evermore" (2 Samuel 22:51), echoing the Davidic covenant of 2 Samuel 7:12-16 and ultimately pointing to Christ the Son of David (Matthew 1:1; Luke 1:32-33).

kids.question: What are the words called at the beginning of chapter 23?
kids.explanation: Now these be the last words of David (2 Samuel 23:1).

kids.question: What did three of David's mighty men do to bring him water from the well of Bethlehem?
kids.option_a: They broke through the host of the Philistines and drew water out of the well of Bethlehem
kids.option_b: They dug a new well near David's camp
kids.option_c: They carried water from the Jordan river for David
kids.option_d: They prayed and water sprang up from the ground
kids.explanation: Three mighty men brake through the host of the Philistines, and drew water out of the well of Bethlehem, that was by the gate, and took it, and brought it to David (2 Samuel 23:16).

kids.question: What did David do with the water the three mighty men brought him from Bethlehem?
kids.option_a: He poured it out unto the LORD and would not drink it
kids.option_b: He shared it with all his soldiers
kids.option_c: He offered it to the wounded first
kids.option_d: He drank it and praised God
kids.explanation: David would not drink thereof, but poured it out unto the LORD (2 Samuel 23:16).

teens.question: What does David say a just ruler is like in his last words?
teens.option_a: He that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God, and he shall be as the light of the morning when the sun riseth, even a morning without clouds
teens.option_b: He that ruleth over men shall be strong as an ox and swift as an eagle
teens.option_c: A righteous king is like a strong tower to which the poor man runs
teens.option_d: The just ruler shall be like a fruitful vine and his kingdom shall flourish
teens.explanation: David said, "He that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God. And he shall be as the light of the morning, when the sun riseth, even a morning without clouds" (2 Samuel 23:3-4).

teens.question: What was the exploit of Adino the Eznite, chief of the three mighty men?
teens.option_a: He lifted up his spear against eight hundred, whom he slew at one time
teens.option_b: He fought seven giants in a single battle
teens.option_c: He held a mountain pass alone for three days
teens.option_d: He defeated the Philistine commander in single combat
teens.explanation: These be the names of the mighty men whom David had: The Tachmonite that sat in the seat...the same was Adino the Eznite: he lift up his spear against eight hundred, whom he slew at one time (2 Samuel 23:8).

teens.question: What did Eleazar the son of Dodo do in the great battle against the Philistines?
teens.option_a: He arose and smote the Philistines until his hand was weary and his hand clave unto the sword, and the LORD wrought a great victory that day
teens.option_b: He rallied the retreating troops and charged the Philistine line
teens.option_c: He killed their commander and took his armor as a trophy
teens.option_d: He alone held the city gate while David's men regrouped
teens.explanation: Eleazar arose, and smote the Philistines until his hand was weary, and his hand clave unto the sword: and the LORD wrought a great victory that day (2 Samuel 23:10).

teens.question: What did Shammah the son of Agee do in the lentil field while the rest of the people fled?
teens.option_a: He stood in the midst of the ground, and defended it, and slew the Philistines: and the LORD wrought a great victory
teens.option_b: He retreated to a hill and fought the Philistines in a narrow pass
teens.option_c: He rallied the people and organized a counterattack
teens.option_d: He single-handedly captured the Philistine commander
teens.explanation: When the people fled from the Philistines, Shammah stood in the midst of the ground, and defended it, and slew the Philistines: and the LORD wrought a great victory (2 Samuel 23:11-12).

teens.question: Who was Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and what were two of his famous deeds?
teens.option_a: He slew two lionlike men of Moab, and went down and slew a lion in a pit on a snowy day, and also slew an Egyptian with the Egyptian's own spear
teens.option_b: He killed seven Philistine giants in the valley of Rephaim
teens.option_c: He defended Jerusalem single-handedly when Absalom's army first approached
teens.option_d: He swam the flooded Jordan to deliver David's message to Zadok the priest
teens.explanation: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada slew two lionlike men of Moab: he went down also and slew a lion in the midst of a pit in time of snow: And he slew an Egyptian, a goodly man: and the Egyptian had a spear in his hand...and he plucked the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and slew him with his own spear (2 Samuel 23:20-21).

adults.question: How does David describe the inspiration of his last words?
adults.option_a: "The Spirit of the LORD spake by me, and his word was in my tongue"
adults.option_b: "The angel of the LORD appeared to me and gave me these words"
adults.option_c: "God spoke to me in a vision of the night"
adults.option_d: "I have written these words after many years of meditation on the law"
adults.explanation: David said, "The Spirit of the LORD spake by me, and his word was in my tongue" (2 Samuel 23:2), asserting divine inspiration for his last oracle.

adults.question: What does David say about his everlasting covenant with God in his last words?
adults.option_a: "Although my house be not so with God; yet he hath made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things, and sure"
adults.option_b: "God has confirmed my house will stand forever without any wavering"
adults.option_c: "My sons shall sit upon the throne of Israel for a thousand generations"
adults.option_d: "God has written my covenant on tablets of stone to endure forever"
adults.explanation: David said, "Although my house be not so with God; yet he hath made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things, and sure: for this is all my salvation, and all my desire" (2 Samuel 23:5).

adults.question: What does David compare the sons of Belial to in his final oracle?
adults.option_a: "The sons of Belial shall be all of them as thorns thrust away, because they cannot be taken with hands"
adults.option_b: "The wicked are like chaff that the wind drives away"
adults.option_c: "The ungodly are as the waves of the sea casting up mire"
adults.option_d: "Those who reject God are like a broken bow that fails in battle"
adults.explanation: David said, "But the sons of Belial shall be all of them as thorns thrust away, because they cannot be taken with hands: But the man that shall touch them must be fenced with iron and the staff of a spear" (2 Samuel 23:6-7).

adults.question: How does the text describe what the people did after Eleazar's great stand — what was the nature of the victory?
adults.option_a: The people returned after Eleazar only to spoil the dead — they did not share the fighting, only the aftermath
adults.option_b: The people rallied and together finished the routing of the Philistines
adults.option_c: The people carried Eleazar from the field because he was so exhausted
adults.option_d: The people offered sacrifices at the site of the battle in gratitude
adults.explanation: The people returned after him only to spoil (2 Samuel 23:10) — they had fled and came back only to loot the bodies after Eleazar had won the battle alone, highlighting the singular nature of his heroism.

adults.question: Why did David refuse to drink the water the three mighty men brought from Bethlehem?
adults.option_a: He said, "Is not this the blood of the men that went in jeopardy of their lives?" — treating it as sacred because of the life-risk involved
adults.option_b: He said it was a sin to drink water fetched from enemy territory
adults.option_c: He said the LORD had told him in a vision not to drink it
adults.option_d: He gave it to the priests to pour out as a libation offering
adults.explanation: David said, "Is not this the blood of the men that went in jeopardy of their lives?" therefore he would not drink it. These things did these three mighty men (2 Samuel 23:17).

adults.question: How did the rank and achievements of Benaiah compare to the chief three mighty men?
adults.option_a: He was more honourable than the thirty, but he attained not to the first three, and David set him over his guard
adults.option_b: He was equal to the three chiefs and considered the fourth greatest warrior
adults.option_c: He surpassed all three chiefs in personal combat but was ranked lower by David
adults.option_d: He was the most famous of all David's warriors and considered greatest of all
adults.explanation: Benaiah was more honourable than the thirty, but he attained not to the first three. And David set him over his guard (2 Samuel 23:23).

adults.question: Who is listed last among the thirty mighty men, and what is theologically significant about his inclusion?
adults.option_a: Uriah the Hittite is last in the list, and his inclusion is a silent indictment of David — the king had him killed to cover adultery with his wife Bathsheba
adults.option_b: Mephibosheth is last, honoring Jonathan's memory through his son
adults.option_c: Joab is listed last because he was the most loyal of all
adults.option_d: Ittai the Gittite is last because he was a foreigner who earned his place
adults.explanation: The list ends, "Uriah the Hittite: thirty and seven in all" (2 Samuel 23:39). Placing Uriah, whose murder David had arranged (2 Samuel 11:15), last in the list of David's greatest warriors serves as a literary reminder of David's gravest sin against one of his most faithful men.

adults.question: What does the phrase "ordered in all things, and sure" in David's description of the everlasting covenant indicate theologically?
adults.option_a: The covenant is both reliable in its terms (ordered) and unbreakable in its guarantee (sure), reflecting the unconditional Davidic covenant of 2 Samuel 7 that ultimately points to Christ
adults.option_b: It means the covenant included detailed legal stipulations that both parties had to observe
adults.option_c: It reflects the administrative organization of David's kingdom under God's law
adults.option_d: It indicates David personally reviewed and approved every clause of the covenant
adults.explanation: "Ordered in all things, and sure" (2 Samuel 23:5) describes the Davidic covenant as comprehensive and unalterable, echoing 2 Samuel 7:16 ("thy throne shall be established for ever") and pointing forward to Christ of whom Gabriel said, "The LORD God shall give unto him the throne of his father David...and of his kingdom there shall be no end" (Luke 1:32-33).

kids.question: What sin did David commit that brought a plague on Israel?
kids.option_a: He numbered the people of Israel and Judah
kids.option_c: He refused to offer a sacrifice
kids.option_d: He took gold from the temple
kids.explanation: David said to Joab, "Go now through all the tribes of Israel...and number ye the people." And David's heart smote him after that he had numbered the people (2 Samuel 24:2, 10).

kids.question: How many people died in the plague that came as punishment for David's census?
kids.explanation: So the LORD sent a pestilence upon Israel...and there died of the people from Dan even to Beersheba seventy thousand men (2 Samuel 24:15).

kids.question: What did David buy from Araunah the Jebusite to stop the plague?
kids.option_a: The threshingfloor, to build an altar unto the LORD
kids.option_b: A field for the people to hide in
kids.option_c: A cave to place the ark of the LORD
kids.option_d: An ox for the temple priests
kids.explanation: David bought the threshingfloor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver. And David built there an altar unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. So the LORD was intreated for the land, and the plague was stayed from Israel (2 Samuel 24:24-25).

teens.question: What were the three choices of punishment God offered David through Gad the seer?
teens.option_a: Seven years of famine, three months fleeing before enemies, or three days of pestilence
teens.option_b: Three years of famine, seven months of war, or one month of plague
teens.option_c: Ten years of drought, one year of war, or seven days of plague
teens.option_d: Forty years of wandering, seven years of enemies, or one day of the LORD's judgment
teens.explanation: Gad offered David three choices: seven years of famine, three months fleeing before enemies, or three days of pestilence in the land (2 Samuel 24:13).

teens.question: What did David say when choosing between the three punishments?
teens.option_a: "Let us fall now into the hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man"
teens.option_b: "Choose the famine so the army will be preserved to fight our enemies"
teens.option_c: "Let three months of war be the judgment for I trust in my soldiers"
teens.option_d: "Let God decide without my choosing, for all three are too great to bear"
teens.explanation: David said unto Gad, "I am in a great strait: let us fall now into the hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man" (2 Samuel 24:14).

teens.question: What did David see that moved him greatly when the angel was stretching out his hand over Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: He saw the angel of the LORD standing between the earth and heaven, having a drawn sword stretched out over Jerusalem
teens.option_b: He saw a great fire descend from heaven on the city
teens.option_c: He saw a pillar of cloud over the temple mount
teens.option_d: He saw seventy thousand bodies fall to the ground at once
teens.explanation: David lifted up his eyes, and saw the angel of the LORD stand between the earth and the heaven, having a drawn sword in his hand stretched out over Jerusalem (2 Samuel 24:16-17).

teens.question: What did David pray when he saw the angel and the people dying?
teens.option_a: "I have sinned, and I have done wickedly: but these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand, I pray thee, be against me, and against my father's house"
teens.option_b: "LORD have mercy and spare Jerusalem for the sake of thy servant"
teens.option_c: "Remember thy covenant with Abraham and spare Israel thy people"
teens.option_d: "Let the angel pass over and let me bear the punishment alone"
teens.explanation: David spake unto the LORD when he saw the angel that smote the people, and said, "Lo, I have sinned, and I have done wickedly: but these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand, I pray thee, be against me, and against my father's house" (2 Samuel 24:17).

teens.question: What did Araunah offer to give David for free when the king came to buy the threshing floor?
teens.option_a: The threshingfloor, the oxen for burnt offering, the threshing instruments and the oxen's yokes for wood — all freely
teens.option_b: Only the threshing floor itself as a gift
teens.option_c: A portion of the land for a small payment
teens.option_d: The oxen but not the threshing floor itself
teens.explanation: Araunah said, "Let my lord the king take and offer up what seemeth good unto him: behold, here be oxen for burnt sacrifice, and threshing instruments and other instruments of the oxen for wood." All these things did Araunah...give unto the king (2 Samuel 24:22-23).

adults.question: Why was God angry against Israel at the beginning of chapter 24, and how does this make the narrative theologically complex?
adults.option_a: The text says God's anger moved David to number Israel, yet David is punished for it — showing divine sovereignty and human responsibility operating simultaneously in the same act
adults.option_b: God was angry because David had failed to complete the building of the temple
adults.option_c: God was angry at Israel's idolatry which David then compounded with the census
adults.option_d: God was angry because David had broken his covenant with Mephibosheth
adults.explanation: "And again the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he moved David against them to say, Go, number Israel and Judah" (2 Samuel 24:1); yet David is then held responsible (24:10) — a theological mystery that 1 Chronicles 21:1 clarifies by attributing the instigation to Satan, showing the interaction of divine permissive will and human sin.

adults.question: What was Joab's objection to David's census command?
adults.option_a: Joab asked why the king delighted in such a thing and said it would be a cause of trespass to Israel
adults.option_b: Joab said the army could not be spared from their posts to conduct a census
adults.option_c: Joab said Moses had commanded no census be taken except by divine command
adults.option_d: Joab said the people would revolt if forced to register for a census
adults.explanation: Joab said to the king, "Now the LORD thy God add unto the people...but why doth my lord the king delight in this thing?" (2 Samuel 24:3), implying the census was an act of pride or distrust of God's promise.

adults.question: What was the total count of Israel and Judah when the census was complete?
adults.option_a: Eight hundred thousand valiant men in Israel and five hundred thousand men of Judah
adults.option_b: One million men in Israel and three hundred thousand in Judah
adults.option_c: Six hundred thousand in Israel and two hundred thousand in Judah
adults.option_d: One million five hundred thousand in total across both kingdoms
adults.explanation: The number of the people was in Israel eight hundred thousand valiant men that drew the sword; and the men of Judah were five hundred thousand men (2 Samuel 24:9).

adults.question: What was the theological sin in David's census, according to the broader biblical context?
adults.option_a: The census reflected pride in military strength and trust in numbers rather than in the LORD, violating the principle that Israel's security rested on God alone and not on the size of its army
adults.option_b: The census violated the Mosaic law requiring a half-shekel atonement payment for each man counted
adults.option_c: The census was sinful because it was conducted in the seventh year which belonged to the LORD
adults.option_d: David had not received a direct divine command to count, which was required before any census
adults.explanation: David's own heart condemned him (2 Samuel 24:10); the census likely reflected trust in military numbers rather than God. Exodus 30:12 also required a ransom payment for each counted man "that there be no plague among them," which David apparently failed to observe.

adults.question: Where was the destroying angel when God commanded him to hold his hand, and what is the theological significance of that location?
adults.option_a: The angel was by the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite on Mount Moriah — the future site of the temple — and this divine arrest at that location consecrated the site for the permanent sanctuary
adults.option_b: The angel stood at the gates of Jerusalem and turned back at David's prayer
adults.option_c: The angel halted at the Jordan River when David offered sacrifice
adults.option_d: The angel was restrained at the border of Judah and Benjamin by a heavenly command
adults.explanation: The angel was by the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite (2 Samuel 24:16); the LORD commanded him to hold his hand there, and David purchased the site and built an altar (24:18-25); 2 Chronicles 3:1 identifies this as Mount Moriah, the very site where Solomon later built the temple.

adults.question: Why did David refuse Araunah's offer to give him the threshing floor and oxen for free?
adults.option_a: He said he would not offer burnt offerings to the LORD which cost him nothing, and insisted on paying fifty shekels of silver for the threshing floor and oxen
adults.option_b: He refused because the law required personal purchase for a sacrifice site
adults.option_c: He refused because he wanted to honor Araunah with a greater sum
adults.option_d: He refused because the land belonged originally to the tribe of Judah
adults.explanation: David said to Araunah, "Nay; but I will surely buy it of thee at a price: neither will I offer burnt offerings unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me nothing." So David bought the threshingfloor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver (2 Samuel 24:24).

adults.question: How does the purchase of Araunah's threshing floor parallel Abraham's purchase of the cave of Machpelah?
adults.option_a: Both involve a patriarch insisting on paying full price for sacred land from a Gentile owner rather than accepting it as a gift, establishing a permanent legal claim through purchase
adults.option_b: Both sites were later destroyed by enemies of Israel and had to be repurchased
adults.option_c: Both purchases were witnessed by the same number of elders under the same legal protocol
adults.option_d: Both sites were on Mount Moriah and were consecrated in the same ceremony
adults.explanation: Abraham refused Ephron's offer to give him Machpelah for free and insisted on paying full price (Genesis 23:13); David refused Araunah's offer and insisted on paying for the threshing floor (2 Samuel 24:24) — both establishing incontestable legal ownership of sacred sites through voluntary, witnessed purchase from Gentile owners.

adults.question: How does the ending of 2 Samuel — with the plague stayed through David's sacrifice on the future temple site — serve as a theological bridge to 1 Kings and the building of the temple?
adults.option_a: It shows that the site of the permanent temple was identified and consecrated through David's intercessory sacrifice, and that the temple's purpose was atonement — staying the judgment of God — even before Solomon built it
adults.option_b: It shows that Solomon chose the temple site based on David's military victories in that valley
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that the plague proved Israel was finally united enough for a permanent sanctuary to be built
adults.option_d: It shows that God chose the temple site to honor Araunah for his generosity toward David
adults.explanation: God halted the destroying angel at the threshing floor of Araunah on Mount Moriah (2 Samuel 24:16); David purchased it and sacrificed there, and the plague was stayed (24:25). 1 Chronicles 22:1 records David declaring, "This is the house of the LORD God, and this is the altar of the burnt offering for Israel," directly connecting the atonement sacrifice of 2 Samuel 24 to the temple that Solomon would build on the same spot (2 Chronicles 3:1).
